It should noted that among the Arabs, families and tribes maintained deep alliances whose responsibilities were given great significance. Here, after the general declaration of disassociation, Muslims are instructed not to let any such alliance obstruct their collective Jihad against the polytheists, but to declare war against all of them.

اِنَّمَا النَّسِيْۗءُ زِيَادَةٌ فِي الْكُفْرِ يُضَلُّ بِهِ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا يُحِلُّوْنَهٗ عَامًا وَّيُحَرِّمُوْنَهٗ عَامًا لِّيُوَاطِــــُٔــوْا عِدَّةَ مَا حَرَّمَ اللّٰهُ فَيُحِلُّوْا مَا حَرَّمَ اللّٰهُ ۭ زُيِّنَ لَهُمْ سُوْۗءُ اَعْمَالِهِمْ وَاللّٰهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكٰفِرِيْنَ 

**The Nature of Intercalation (Nasi'):** 'Indeed, the postponing [of restriction] is an increase in disbelief by which those who have disbelieved are led astray.' *Nasī'* means to postpone or push back. *Nasā'a al-ba'īr* means: he drove the camel away from the well. *Nasā'a al-rā'ī* means: the shepherd postponed watering the camels. From this, the noun *nasī'* was formed, which in the custom of the pagan Arabs referred to the month which was postponed, adding days to its calendar. The solar year is around eleven days longer than the lunar year. To adjust the lunar year to this difference, the Arabs added days, which practically meant adding three months every eight years—meaning: a leap month was added every second or third year. Through this, they assumed they were respecting the sacred months, while securing their commercial interests by keeping them in the same season. The Arabs did this under the concept of fulfilling their religious duty and protecting their interests, but the Quran did not accept this, declaring it an increase in disbelief that led them further away from the straight path of Abraham's religion.

'They make it lawful one year and forbid it another to adjust the number of what Allah has forbidden and [thus] make lawful what Allah has forbidden.' This explains why this is an increase in disbelief: because although the number of sacred months is maintained, those months declared sacred by Allah become sometimes sacred and sometimes not, whereas those months declared sacred by Allah are the only sacred months, which can neither be replaced nor their respect transferred to another month. It should noted that adjusting the lunar year to the solar year in this manner removed the season of Hajj and the sacred months from their original places, returning to them only once every 33 years. Divine decree arranged that during the Farewell Pilgrimage, the Hajj fell exactly on its original lunar and Abrahimi date. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) announced: 'Time has completed its cycle, returning to its original state on the day Allah created the heavens and the earth.' These words are a reflection of the verse: 

'Indeed, the number of months with Allah is twelve months in the register of Allah [from] the day He created the heavens and the earth; of these, four are sacred.'

'made pleasing to them is the evil of their deeds. And Allah does not guide the disbelieving people.' That is: this mixture of disbelief and Islam they chose is not a religious deed, but is their evil deed made pleasing to them, which they take for religion. Allah will not make such disbelievers successful. Such religion will go to waste on the Day of Judgment.

The upcoming subject (Verses 38 to 42): Having commanded war against the polytheists and the People of the Book, the discourse now turned toward the hypocrites, pursuing them until the end of the Surah. Thus, after the open enemies, attention was turned to the hidden enemies, exposing their every weakness and mischief so the Muslims would remain alert. While the previous comments on their behavior were soft, in this Surah, a decisive judgment is announced regarding them, so those among them who possess some capacity for reform may reform to become sincere members of the society, and those who are dead may be cleared out. Recite the verses:

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوْا مَا لَكُمْ اِذَا قِيْلَ لَكُمُ انْفِرُوْا فِيْ سَبِيْلِ اللّٰهِ اثَّاقَلْتُمْ اِلَى الْاَرْضِ ۭ اَرَضِيْتُمْ بِالْحَيٰوةِ الدُّنْيَا مِنَ الْاٰخِرَةِ ۚ فَمَا مَتَاعُ الْحَيٰوةِ الدُّنْيَا فِي الْاٰخِرَةِ اِلَّا قَلِيْلٌ 

*Nafār/Nafūr/Nafīr* means going out or rising for war or a similar purpose. *Tathāqul/Iththāqul* means considering something heavy and becoming listless. Stating 'to the earth' (*ilā al-ard*) alongside it draws a vivid picture: when you are commanded to rise for war, you collapse listless to the earth as if a heavy burden broke your back.

**Addressing the Hypocrites:** Although the address is general, the focus of the discourse is on those hypocrites who, out of love for ease and interests, were avoiding the general call to Jihad. He stated: What has happened to you that you claim faith, yet when its obvious demand arises, you find it so heavy to rise?

**The Core Disease of the Hypocrites is the Love of the World:** 'Are you satisfied with the life of this world rather than the Hereafter? But what is the enjoyment of worldly life compared to the Hereafter except a [very] little.' This is a question pointing to their core disease: they became satisfied with this worldly life, its pleasures, and its ease, ignoring the Hereafter. They do not wish to lose this present ease for a future promise, whereas in the Hereafter, when reality is laid bare, they will realize what a valueless thing they chose at the cost of an eternal kingdom.

**Warning to the Hypocrites:** 'If you do not go forth, He will punish you with a painful punishment and will replace you with another people, and you will not harm Him at all. And Allah is over all things competent.' This is a threat to the hypocrites: if you do not rise for Jihad, He will apprehend you with His punishment, and do not assume that His work will stop because of you. Your rising is for your own success. God's work is not dependent on you; He will stand in your place other servants who will not be listless, coward, and self-interested like you. In Surah Muhammad: 'And if you turn away, He will replace you with another people; then they will not be the likes of you.' (Muhammad: 38).

**The Independence of Allah:** 'And Allah is over all things competent' (*wa-Allāhu ʿalā kulli šay'in qadīr*) points to multiple realities: first, He is competent to punish you; second, He is competent to replace you with others; third, He is competent to bring His schemes to fruition, being independent of anyone in accomplishing His intentions.

The upcoming context shows that the object pronoun in 'If you do not aid him' (*illā tanṣurūhu*) returns to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), as he was the one who called people to Jihad as God's representative.

**God's Help Accompanying the Prophet:** 'If you do not aid him - Allah has already aided him when those who disbelieved had driven him out [of Makkah] as one of two, when they were in the cave and he said to his companion, "Do not grieve; indeed, Allah is with us." Then Allah sent down His tranquility upon him and supported him with angels you did not see and made the word of those who disbelieved the lowest, while the word of Allah - that is the highest. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.'

This is further clarification of the independence of Allah from these hypocrites: if you do not support the Prophet in this work, do not assume that this work will stop. The God who helped him at every step since the emigration is still here to help him. Recall the day when the disbelievers drove him out of Makkah as one of two (this refers to the emigration journey when only Hazrat Abu Bakr Al-Siddiq was with him, and all the Quraysh were seeking his blood, yet he emerged throwing dust into their eyes and no one could harm him). Recall when they were in the Cave of Thawr, and the enemy reached the mouth of the cave. To the extent that his companion feared that they were caught, and a danger arose for the Prophet's life; but the Prophet reassured him: 'Do not grieve; indeed, Allah is with us,' and Allah sent down His tranquility upon him. Recall those many battles where His unseen armies accompanied him, until disbelief was subdued and the religion was elevated. Reflect: can this Prophet, whom God helped in this manner, be in need of your help? His God is Exalted in Might and Wise; He accomplishes whatever He intends, and His every action is based on wisdom.

**Lack of Means is No Excuse in Jihad:** 'Go forth, whether light or heavy, and strive with your wealth and your lives in the cause of Allah. That is better for you, if you only knew.'

*Khifāf* is the plural of *khafīf* (light), and *thiqāl* is the plural of *thaqīl* (heavy). Here, *khafīf* refers to the one who, due to poverty, lacks weapons, means, and provisions; *thaqīl* refers to his contrary—namely: the one who is fully equipped. The meaning is: do not make the lack of means an excuse to avoid Jihad. With whatever means are available, small or large, rise to strive with your wealth and lives in the cause of Allah. This is better for you, if you only knew its blessings and benefits, and knew the loss of being deprived of them.

The word *šuqqah* refers to the distance traveled by a traveler. Stating 'the journey was difficult [i.e., distant] for them' (*baʿudat ʿalayhimu al-šuqqah*) points to the Battle of Tabuk, in which the Muslims faced the test of a distant journey alongside a harsh season. This battle took place in Rajab, 9 AH. The season was hot, crops were ready for harvest, the distance was long, and the face-off was with a massive, organized army; consequently, the hypocrites' weakness was completely exposed. They avoided the battle, inventing false excuses. Although the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) knew their excuses were false, he overlooked them out of his generosity; but Allah exposed them so they would reform if they wished, or at least the Muslims would remain safe from them. He stated: if they had expected some easy gain and a short journey, they would have followed you; but the distance was long, and their resolve failed. Now, they will swear by Allah to reassure you: 'If we could, we would have gone out with you.' He stated: 'They destroy themselves' by inventing these false excuses, because avoiding the general call to Jihad brings a severe punishment. Under Al-Baqarah verse 195, we explained that fleeing from the duties of God is equivalent to throwing oneself into destruction.

The upcoming subject (Verses 43 to 60): In the subsequent verses, He rebuked the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in a highly gentle manner for his practice of accepting the hypocrites' excuses out of his generosity. Then, He described a criterion to distinguish between the sincere and the hypocrites: sincere believers will never seek permission to avoid Jihad; those who seek permission are only those who have no faith in Allah and the Last Day, and whose hearts are in doubt, because of which Allah chose to deprive them of this blessing. Then, He consoled the Muslims: it was better that these hypocrites did not join you, because if they had, they would have caused division and mischief within your ranks, as they had done in the past; they are, after all, agents of others. Afterward, He pointed to certain hypocrites who had presented a 'pious' excuse—claiming that they were avoiding the battle to protect their chastity from being tempted by Roman women. He rebuked their false piety, exposing the malice in their hearts.

Then, He warned the hypocrites: none of your spending is accepted by God, because you are devoid of faith; your spending is under compulsion, and your prayers are for show. Along with this, He instructed the Prophet: pay no heed to their wealth, which will only serve as a source of punishment for them in this world and the Hereafter.

Afterward, He stated: their remaining with you is based on fear or greed; since they have no way of escape, they are trapped with you. Their greed is clear from their slandering you regarding the distribution of charity. He followed this by explaining the designated avenues of charity, so no unqualified person would attempt to partake of this wealth. Recite the verses:

عَفَا اللّٰهُ عَنْكَ ۚ لِمَ اَذِنْتَ لَهُمْ حَتّٰي يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكَ الَّذِيْنَ صَدَقُوْا وَتَعْلَمَ الْكٰذِبِيْنَ 

**Exploiting the Prophet's Generosity:** Overlooking and pardoning is an inseparable requirement of generosity. Just as the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was the embodiment of all high human qualities, he possessed the quality of overlooking in the highest degree. The hypocrites attempted to exploit this: to avoid their duties—especially Jihad—they would invent false excuses, seeking permission to remain at home. The Prophet knew their excuses were false, but based on his generosity, he would overlook them and grant them permission. Since this permission served to cover up their hypocrisy, making them bolder in their deceit, Allah warned the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). But the tone of this warning is highly gentle, beginning with the declaration of forgiveness to show that the purpose was not rebuke, but drawing attention to how the hypocrites were exploiting his generosity. He stated: out of your generosity, you grant them permission despite knowing their excuses are false; had you not, they would have been exposed, and the truthful would have been distinguished from the liars. Your permission serves as a cover for them.

Commentary on Verses 44-45:

لَا يَسْتَاْذِنُكَ الَّذِيْنَ يُؤْمِنُوْنَ بِاللّٰهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْاٰخِرِ اَنْ يُّجَاهِدُوْا بِاَمْوَالِهِمْ وَاَنْفُسِهِمْ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ عَلِيْمٌۢ بِالْمُتَّقِيْنَ ۔ اِنَّمَا يَسْـتَاْذِنُكَ الَّذِيْنَ لَا يُؤْمِنُوْنَ بِاللّٰهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْاٰخِرِ وَارْتَابَتْ قُلُوْبُهُمْ فَهُمْ فِيْ رَيْبِهِمْ يَتَرَدَّدُوْنَ 

**Clear Difference Between the Sincere and the Hypocrites:** This describes a clear difference between the sincere and the hypocrites: those who believe in Allah and the Last Day never seek permission to avoid Jihad; Allah is aware of His righteous servants. Those who seek permission are only those who have no faith in Allah and the Last Day, and whose hearts are in doubt, because of which they claim faith with their tongues while their hearts are hesitant. It should noted that what is under discussion here is those who presented false excuses though they could not satisfy others, because the excuses that make a person unable to join Jihad are obvious. Those who are truly unable have clear excuses, and everyone accepts them. Indeed, ahead in this Surah, He described these true unable ones:

'There is not upon the weak or upon the ill or upon those who do not find anything to spend any discomfort when they are sincere to Allah and His Messenger. There is not against the doers of good any cause [for blame]. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. Nor [is there blame] upon those who, when they came to you that you might give them mounts, you said, "I do not find anything for you to ride upon." They turned back while their eyes overflowed with tears of grief that they did not find anything to spend.' (At-Tawbah: 91-92).

I repeatedly think that 'Allah is aware of His righteous servants' (*wa-Allāhu ʿalīmun bi-al-muttaqīn*) points to these sincere unable ones.

وَلَوْ اَرَادُوا الْخُرُوْجَ لَاَعَدُّوْا لَهٗ عُدَّةً وَّلٰكِنْ كَرِهَ اللّٰه انْۢبِعَاثَهُمْ فَثَبَّطَهُمْ وَقِيْلَ اقْعُدُوْا مَعَ الْقٰعِدِيْنَ 

**Facilitation is in Proportion to Resolve:** That is: all their excuses are false. If they had intended to join, they would have made some preparation. Preparing some weapons and provisions according to their capability was not difficult for them; but they lacked the resolve, and the law of God is that He does not grant facilitation to those who do not rise on their own to do good. Since they chose to remain at home like children, women, and the unable, God also deprived them of resolve.

**The Divine Law Concerning Facilitation:** *Tathbīt* means to hold someone back from a task. This describes the divine law concerning facilitation, which we have explained in multiple places. 'and it was said, "Sit with those who sit"' is a tone of rebuke—meaning: since you are empty of resolve to enter the field, go and sit with those who remain at home. Ahead, this rebuke becomes even sharper.

Commentary on Verses 47-48:

لَوْ خَرَجُوْا فِيْكُمْ مَّا زَادُوْكُمْ اِلَّا خَبَالًا وَّلَا۟اَوْضَعُوْا خِلٰلَكُمْ يَبْغُوْنَكُمُ الْفِتْنَةَ ۚ وَفِيْكُمْ سَمّٰعُوْنَ لَهُمْ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ عَلِيْمٌۢ بِالظّٰلِمِيْنَ ۔ لَقَدِ ابْتَغَوُا الْفِتْنَةَ مِنْ قَبْلِ وَقَلَّبُوْا لَكَ الْاُمُوْرَ حَتّٰي جَاۗءَ الْحَقُّ وَظَهَرَ اَمْرُ اللّٰهِ وَهُمْ كٰرِهُوْنَ 

*Khabāl* means corruption and harm; *īḍāʿ* means running and rushing. The meaning is: it was better that these people did not join you. If they had, they would have caused corruption within your ranks, and their effort would have been directed toward causing some trial among you. 'and among you are listeners to them' (*wa-fīkum sammāʿūna lahum*) points to those simple-minded Muslims who, though not hypocrites, were easily deceived by the hypocrites. Through these words, He subtly warned the Muslims to remain alert against those within their ranks who are highly sensitive and easily affected by the mischief-makers. *Sammā'ūn* was discussed under Al-Ma'idah verse 41. 'And Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers' is a threat—meaning: Allah is aware of these wrongdoers, and they will face their punishment.

'They had already desired dissension before and had upset matters for you until the truth came and the decree of Allah appeared, though they disliked it.' **Pointing to the Past Mischiefs of the Hypocrites:**

This refers to those past mischiefs and plots which the hypocrites carried out to harm the Muslims. 'and had upset matters for you' (*wa-qallabū laka al-umūr*)—meaning: they attempted to present matters in a distorted light through their fanned rumors. The Quran confined itself to a brief indication because these matters were well known to the Muslims. These were the ones who, as detailed under Al-Anfal, attempted to turn the Muslims toward the commercial caravan despite the Prophet's intent being clear; these were the ones who, during Uhud, first advised fighting within the city, and when their advice was rejected, Abdullah bin Ubayy withdrew with his 300 men, and then spread the rumor that the Prophet was a bad-wisher who brought his nation to destruction; these were the ones who, during the Battle of Al-Muraysi', fanned rumors that almost caused a clash between the Ansar and Mahajirun; and during this, they fanned the rumor of slander (*ifk*) against Hazrat Aisha; and during Hunayn, they fanned doubts regarding the distribution of spoils. In short, they spared no effort to cause division, but Allah protected the Muslims from their trials.

**Hypocrisy in the Garb of Piety:** 'And among them is he who says, "Permit me [to remain at home] and do not tempt me." Unquestionably, into trial they have fallen. And indeed, Hell will encompass the disbelievers.' This refers to those hypocrites who presented a 'pious' excuse to avoid Tabuk—claiming that they were weak regarding women, and wished to avoid the battle lest they be tempted by Roman women. Although only a few must have presented this excuse, it was an excuse wrapped in a garb of piety; therefore, the Quran mentioned it separately to warn the Muslims against a specific tool of Satan: sometimes, he attacks in the garb of piety. This is similar to the excuse some innovators present to avoid congregational prayers—claiming they avoid the mosque to protect themselves from show (*riyā'*). 'Unquestionably, into trial they have fallen' (*alā fī al-fitnati saqatū*) is a highly eloquent segment—meaning: being tempted at Tabuk was a distant thing; they fell into trial right in their homes. For avoiding their duties through such excuses is in itself a trial after which no other trial is needed to ruin them. Stating 'And indeed, Hell will encompass the disbelievers' reveals, first, that such excuse-makers are disbelievers; second, that they may choose whatever excuse to escape, but Hell encompasses them from all sides. They cannot escape.

**The True Inner State of the Hypocrites:** This exposes their true inner state—meaning: excuses are merely a cover; their hearts are filled with malice against you. 'If good befalls you, it distresses them'—when you attain victory, they feel grieved; 'but if disaster strikes you, they say, "We took our matter [i.e., precaution] beforehand," and turn away while they are rejoicing.' We consider 'We took our precaution' (*qad akhadhnā amranā*) as meaning 'We took our security beforehand'.

Commentary on Verses 51-52:

**For the Believers, Both Calamity and Victory are Good:** This answers their joy over any setback: tell them that nothing will strike us except what God has written for us, He is our Ally, and we rely on Him. We believe that whatever He has written for us contains our well-being in this world and the Hereafter. For a believer, both calamity and victory, grief and joy, life and death contain goodness: calamity teaches patience, reform, and turning to God; victory teaches gratitude, performing duties, and excellence. If a believer fights, he is a warrior; if he dies, he is a martyr. Your case is the contrary: because of the path you chose, we expect one of two things for you: either Allah will punish you from Himself, or He will punish you through our hands; and neither contains any good for you. Therefore, wait for what you expect for us, and we are waiting for one of these two things for you. This waiting is not out of malice, but is waiting for the natural outcome of completing the proof. This will be discussed under Surah Nuh.

Commentary on Verses 53-54:

'Say, "Spend willingly or unwillingly; never will it be accepted from you. Indeed, you have been a defiantly disobedient people."' This is an expression of dislike and aversion toward the hypocrites. He stated: tell them that none of your spending, whether willingly or unwillingly, is accepted by God. Spending is accepted only from those loyal to Him. Those who are covenant-breakers and betrayers, spending merely for show, have no value before God. God is not in need of anyone's wealth to accept it. He accepts only the spending of those who serve His religion with true faith and complete sincerity. 'And what prevents their expenditures from being accepted from them...' explains 'Indeed, you have been a defiantly disobedient people.' The meaning is: they are deniers of Allah and His Messenger. They do not fulfill any requirement of faith. If they come to prayer, they come lazily and for show, because they know that if they do not, they cannot count themselves among the Muslims. Similarly, if they spend, they do so unwillingly and for show, so they are counted among the Muslims. It should noted that just as their prayer is compulsory and for show, their spending is unwilling and for show, and God accepts no such deed. Take a look at what we wrote under Surah An-Nisa, verse 142. We explained there that their prayer was not a prayer, but a performance to deceive the Muslims. Obviously, such prayer will be lazy, empty of devotion and humility.

**Wealth and Children Receive Value Alongside Faith:** Those blessed with wealth and children can serve as a great asset for the nation if they are also blessed with faith and sincerity. Every well-wisher of the nation looks upon them with respect, because the service they can perform through their wealth and lives is beyond the reach of others. Among the hypocrites, there were those possessing wealth and children who could have performed great services and attained high ranks, and indeed the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) desired that they be blessed with faith and sincerity to fulfill the right of these blessings; but when they proved false, Allah informed the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): 'So let not their wealth or their children impress you. Allah only intends to punish them through them in worldly life and that their souls should depart while they are disbelievers.' These are not means of elevation, but are loops of slavery. Because of them, they will be punished in this world, and their death will be in the state of disbelief.

**The Trap of Wealth and Children:** The punishment in this world refers to that punishment decreed for the deniers of the Messenger, as detailed previously. Obviously, if these hypocrites chose the same path, they would face the same end. 'that their souls should depart' (*ta-zhaqa anfusuhum*) shows that those who turn wealth and children into a trap for themselves die with their souls departing in pain, and this trap prevents them from returning to the path of faith. 'Allah only intends' (*innamā yurīdu Allāhu*) points to that divine law which we have explained in multiple places.

Commentary on Verses 56-57:

'And they swear by Allah that they are indeed of you.' Swearing oaths (*ḥalaf*) generally does not appear in a good sense in the Quran. We explained elsewhere that those who lack the proof of character often rely on false oaths to prove themselves trustworthy. Thus, the Quran has shown that the hypocrites attempt to fill their moral void through false oaths. To satisfy the Muslims, they swear: 'We are indeed of you; let no one doubt us.' The Quran stated: 'but they are not of you, but they are a people who are afraid.' They remain with you solely out of fear, as they have no way of escape. The fate of the polytheists and the Jews is before them. Where can they go? If they leave, their interests are at stake. Therefore, they are trapped with you. *Yafraqūna* means fearing and being terrified. *Faruqah* means a coward.

'If they could find a refuge or caves or a place to enter [and hide], they would turn to it while they run headwild.' This explains the preceding statement: if they could find a refuge, cave, or house where they could remain safe alongside their interests, they would not remain with you for even a single day; rather, they would run headwild. *Jamaḥa al-faras* means: the horse became uncontrolled by its rider and ran headwild. That is: you would attempt to hold them, but they would run away; but fortunately, they can find no such place, which is why fear and cowardice have bound them to you.

Commentary on Verses 58-59:

**The Case of the Poor Hypocrites:** Above, the case of the rich hypocrites was described. Now, the case of the poor hypocrites is described: 'And among them are some who criticize you concerning the [distribution of] charities. If they are given from them, they are pleased; but if they are not given from them, at once they are angry.' Just as fear has bound them to you, greed has bound them. If you (the address is to the Prophet) give them from the charities, they remain pleased; but if you reduce their share, they begin criticizing you—claiming that others are given with liberality while they are deprived of their right.

**The Right Path:** 'And if only they had been satisfied with what Allah and His Messenger gave them and said, "Sufficient for us is Allah; Allah will give us of His bounty, and [so will] His Messenger; indeed, to Allah we are supplicants."' This teaches them the right path—namely: relying on Allah and being satisfied. He stated: if they had been satisfied with what Allah and His Messenger gave them, placing their trust in Allah and expecting His further bounty, choosing trust instead of criticism and complaint, it would have been better for them. This would have opened the doors of Allah's further bounty and the Prophet's further concern; but they chose to criticize. It is well known in eloquent Arabic that in such conditional sentences, the response (*ǧawāb*) is often omitted to highlight the intensity, regret, or advice. We have opened this omitted response in the translation.

**The True Recipients of Charity:** Now, He described the recipients of charity, so those unqualified hypocrites who wished to partake of this wealth out of greed would realize who the true recipients are. This subtly indicates that these hypocrites who criticized the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) regarding the distribution of charity were like uninvited guests who wished to share in this wealth without any qualification.

**The Difference Between 'Sadaqah' and 'Zakat':** *Sadaqāt* (charity) is more general than zakat. It includes all spending performed with the intention of seeking reward—whether it is the compulsory zakat or voluntary spending. Since this spending proves the truth of a person's faith, it is called *sadaqah*, whose root is *sidq*—meaning: complete alignment between words and deeds. The following recipients are described here:

**The Meaning of the Poor (Fuqara') and the Needy (Masakin):** *Fuqarā'* and *masākīn* are similar in that both apply to the poor and needy; however, there is some difference between them. 'The poor' (*faqīr*) is the contrary of 'the rich' (*ġanī*)—for example: 'Whether he is rich or poor, Allah is more worthy of them both.' (An-Nisa: 135). Therefore, every needy person is *faqīr*, whether he asks of people or avoids asking to preserve his self-respect. In Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 273, the word *fuqarā'* is used for those who avoid asking. 'The needy' (*miskīn*) is used for the one who, due to poverty or lack of resolve, is unable to strive for his living, relying solely on others. Thus, *miskīn* is a more intense term than *faqīr*. 'and those employed to collect them' (*wa-al-ʿāmilīna ʿalayhā*) refers to those appointed by the government to collect and manage the charity. Their salaries and office expenses are also paid from this fund. 'and for bringing hearts together' (*wa-al-mu'allafati qulūbuhum*) refers to those whose hearts are to be won for the interest of the Islamic state. Sometimes, the government must deal with influential people who are not fully subjects but can harm the Muslims if they join the enemy—especially in border areas. Keeping them in support of the state is a political interest, and this is done by granting them financial support so their sympathies remain with the state. This is a political avenue upon which the state may spend from other resources, or from the charity fund if needed. These can be non-Muslims or nominal Muslims. This also serves to bring them closer to Islam. A group of our jurists considered this avenue terminated after the Prophet's passing or after the dominance of Islam, but this is not highly robust. This is a political avenue that depends on circumstances. Even the strongest state sometimes chooses this method to prevent evil, because resorting to force can raise complex international conflicts. We discussed this in our book *The Islamic State*.

**Clarification of 'Fi al-Riqab':** 'and for freeing captives' (*wa-fī al-riqāb*) contains an omitted noun—namely: *fī fakki al-riqāb* (freeing captives). Freeing slaves is also among the avenues upon which charity can be spent. The use of the preposition *fī* (in/for) shows that tasks that help them gain freedom can be carried out from this fund.

**The Meaning of 'al-Gharimin':** 'and for those in debt' (*wa-al-ġārimīna*). *Garima fī al-tiǧārah* means: he suffered loss in his commerce. *Garāmah* means fine or loss. *Gārim* is the one who, due to loss in business or another cause, falls under such a heavy debt that he cannot settle it on his own. Helping them settle their debts and supporting them is an avenue of charity, so they may rise to serve the society again. It should be kept in mind that in an Islamic state, usury and all unlawful avenues of spending are prohibited; therefore, the question does not arise whether the debt was incurred through some unlawful path. The context shows that *gārim* refers to the one who suffered a setback in his lawful economic efforts or fell under a fine. 'and for the cause of Allah' (*wa-fī sabīli Allāhi*) is a comprehensive term, encompassing Jihad, calling to Islam, and teaching the religion. Based on circumstances, some of these tasks will hold greater importance, but any task through which the religion of Allah is served falls under 'the cause of Allah'.

**The Meaning of 'Ibn al-Sabil':** 'and for the [stranded] traveler' (*wa-ibni al-sabīli*). Mentioning him separately proves that a traveler, by virtue of being a traveler, is entitled to receive help from the charity fund. Traveling can put a person in such a state that—though not poor in his home—he becomes needy in an alien place, unable to manage his needs on his own. Constructing inns, shelters, and providing food and guidance for travelers are among the tasks upon which charity can be spent. This is also under the preposition *fī*, which shows that all tasks of convenience for travelers are included.

**Individual Ownership is Not Necessary for Charity:** A group of our jurists takes the particle *li* in 'Indeed, the charities are for...' (*innamā al-sadaqātu lil...*) as denoting individual ownership (*tamlik*), thereby claiming that charity and zakat funds cannot be spent on the collective well-being of the poor and needy where individual ownership is not established. In our view, this is not based on any strong proof. The particle *li* does not denote only ownership, but carries multiple meanings, including benefit, well-being, and entitlement. Some avenues are under the preposition *fī*, which denotes use and benefit, not ownership. Furthermore, it is obvious that under certain circumstances, greater benefit can be provided to the poor by spending on their collective well-being than by individual ownership; why then should it be limited to individual ownership? We confine ourselves to these indications here.

'an obligation [imposed] by Allah. And Allah is Knowing and Wise.' The structure of this expression was discussed elsewhere: it highlights that this command is compulsory, and is based on knowledge and wisdom.

Commentary on Verses 61-72:

Next, He mentioned some of the mischiefs of the hypocrites, warning them. Then, He compared the character and attributes of both the hypocrites (male and female) and the believers (male and female), so faith and hypocrisy would be clearly distinguished. This comparison is highly helpful in exposing realities, while helping the purpose of reform if used correctly. Recite the verses:

**The Meaning of 'Udhun' (An Ear):** 'And among them are those who abuse the Prophet and say, "He is an ear."' *Udhun* means ear. When used as a metaphor for a person, it carries a sense of subtle sarcasm—meaning: he is merely an ear, listening to and believing everyone. I termed it subtle sarcasm because it is generally used by haters against great men, claiming: he is a great man with many virtues, but he is weak of ear, listening to and believing everyone. It is a sarcasm because listening to everyone, while being a proof of his nobility and generosity, is also a proof of his simplicity and lack of insight.

**The Hypocrites' Sarcasm Against the Prophet:** The hypocrites used this word for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). In their private gatherings, they would mock Allah, His Messenger, and His verses. When this reached the Prophet through some means, and he expressed some anger, the hypocrites would excuse themselves, saying: he is a good man who believes whatever is whispered to him, and becomes suspicious of us who are loyal, whereas we did not utter any word of disrespect intentionally. In his generosity, the Prophet would overlook them. From the perspective of this human nature, the Quran censured them:

**Answering the Hypocrites:** 'Say, "[He is] an ear of goodness for you."' This is the answer: if you consider the Prophet merely an ear, the aspect you harbor is wrong. He is an ear only for your own good. His ears are open to hear your good words, good deeds, and good intentions, and to be pleased by them. His ears are not open to hear your evil reports and whispers. A father is not as concerned for his children as the Prophet is for his Ummah. He seeks only good for you, wishing that his ears receive only good reports regarding you. Therefore, if you consider him an ear, he is so only for your good, and this is a blessing, not something to mock. 

**The Prophet's Trust is in Allah and the Believers:** 'He believes in Allah and believes the believers.' That is: your assumption that he believes everyone is wrong. He believes in Allah, and believes only those reports brought by the sincere believers. If Allah informs him of your state, what report can be truer than that? Similarly, if the believers inform him of your state, why should the Prophet not believe them? The Prophet's sources of information are not rumors, nor is he so simple as to believe rumors; he is guided by God, and listens to the believers. We explained elsewhere that when the verb 'to believe' (*āmana*) is followed by the preposition *li*, it carries the meaning of believing someone's words.

**The Prophet is Absolute Mercy:** 'and [he is] a mercy to those who believe among you.' That is: for those who adopt the path of true faith, the Prophet is absolute mercy and concern. He is not your bad-wisher to open his ears to your enemies; he seeks only your reform and success, and your success in this world and the Hereafter lies in adopting true faith to deserve his mercy and concern. 'Believe' (*āmanū*) is used in its true sense—namely: those who are sincere believers. 'And those who abuse the Messenger of Allah - for them is a painful punishment'—as for those who claim faith only with their tongues while causing grief to the Prophet through their words and actions, they will face a painful punishment.

**An Excuse Worse Than the Crime:** The address is to the Muslims: 'They swear by Allah to you [O believers] to please you. But Allah and His Messenger are more worthy that they should please Him, if they should be believers.' The hypocrites swear false oaths to satisfy you regarding their faith, whereas if they possessed faith, they would care to please Allah and His Messenger rather than satisfying you. This is a highly timely rebuke. The meaning is: this action they choose to prove their faith is in itself proof of their lack of faith. This is similar to the rebuke in 'Unquestionably, into trial they have fallen' above. Sometimes, an excuse is worse than the crime. The path they chose to clear themselves proved them even more guilty: they chose false oaths to present themselves as innocent and the Prophet as weak of ear, instead of seeking forgiveness from God and pleasing the Prophet. This was an active campaign against the Prophet.

Note that the pronoun in 'to please Him' (*an yurḍūhu*) is singular, because pleasing Allah and pleasing the Messenger are one and the same.

*Muhāddah* means to rise in opposition as an enemy.

**Warning the Hypocrites of Punishment:** 'Do they not know that whoever opposes Allah and His Messenger - that for him is the fire of Hell, wherein he will abide eternally? That is the great disgrace.' This is a threat to the hypocrites: instead of asking forgiveness of God and pleasing the Prophet, they have launched a campaign through false oaths to strengthen their group against Allah and His Messenger, so they can carry out their plots. Did they fail to realize after all this reminder that those who oppose Allah and His Messenger will face Hell eternally, and that is the great disgrace? 'That is the great disgrace' subtly indicates: even if they succeed in saving themselves from disgrace in this world through their games, how will they save themselves from this greatest disgrace?

*Had͟hira/Yah͟d͟haru* means fearing and being alert. *Tunazzalu* means revealed.

**The Hypocrites' Fear of Exposure:** This describes the background of the hypocrites' campaign to satisfy the Muslims: up to now, the comments on their behavior were soft, but in this Surah, they felt the tone was decisive, and several of their secrets were discussed; hence, 'The hypocrites fear lest a surah be revealed about them, informing them of what is in their hearts.' Out of this fear, they launched this campaign of false oaths. He stated: tell them that your precautions will not benefit you. Mock Allah, His Messenger, and His verses as you wish; the time has come for Allah to expose all those secrets you fear being exposed. This Surah is the final and decisive Surah regarding the hypocrites, exposing them completely.

**The Meaning of 'Khawd':** *K͟hawḍ* originally means entering water, but regarding talk, it can mean hair-splitting, or moving from one talk to another. We have translated it as 'vain talk'.

'And if you ask them, they will surely say, "We were only conversing and playing."' **Another Excuse Worse Than the Crime:** This describes another of their excuses: if you ask them regarding the mockery they commit in their gatherings against Allah, they will reply: 'Mockery? We were only conversing and playing!' The Quran caught them on this: 'Say, "Is it Allah and His verses and His Messenger that you were mocking?"' Did you find nothing for your play and talk except Allah, His verses, and His Messenger? You play with the beard of your father!

**Mocking the Verses of Allah is Disbelief:** 'Make no excuse; you have disbelieved after your belief.' That is: make no excuse; your excuse is worse than your crime. If a group turns Allah, His verses, and His Messenger into a plaything, what other proof is needed for their crime? This is open disbelief after claiming faith. You claimed faith, which demanded that you prove it through your deeds, but your deeds proved your disbelief, because mocking Allah, His verses, and His Messenger is disbelief. This was discussed under Surah Al-An'am, verse 70.

**Some Dangerous Groups Among the Hypocrites:** 'If We pardon a party of you, We will punish another party because they were criminals.' As was clear from the preceding verses, there were multiple groups of the hypocrites whose standard of hypocrisy and mischief varied. Some groups, as will be clear ahead, were highly dangerous, plotting against Islam in a manner that, if successful, would have been more harmful than the plots of open enemies. Pointing to such groups, He stated: 'If We pardon a party of you'—meaning: if We overlook them in this world, leaving their decision for the Hereafter—'We will punish another party because they were criminals'—meaning: some groups will inevitably face Our punishment in this world, and We will destroy them for their crimes. Ahead, the builders of the Masjid al-Dirar and other groups will be mentioned, along with their fate.

Commentary on Verses 67-70:

**Purifying the Islamic Society of the Hypocrites:** Up to now, the hypocrites remained with the Muslims because of their claim to faith. Now, for the first time, He described their attributes to separate them from the believers, grouping them with the disbelievers in this world and the Hereafter. This is a warning to the hypocrites not to attempt to remain with the Muslims, and an instruction to the Muslims not to let such wolves remain in their flock. This is the first step toward purifying the Islamic society of corrupt elements, indicating that just as the Muslims have declared disassociation from the polytheists and the People of the Book, they must separate those nominal Muslims whose character aligns with that of the polytheists.

**The Same End for Male and Female Hypocrites:** 'The hypocrites, both men and women, are all of a piece.' Here, He explicitly mentioned both male and female hypocrites, declaring them equal in crime, so the women would also realize that they will not escape this end. In religious matters, following others is no excuse; every soul is responsible for its own salvation. This also indicates that women played a major role in this hypocrisy—especially because the love of life and wealth is among the primary causes of hypocrisy, and women play the greatest role in making men prioritize this love. A Hadith records: 'Wives and children make a person a miser and a coward.' The core disease of the hypocrites mentioned above was that the love of wealth and children dominated them in opposition to the demands of religion.

**Every Word of the Hypocrites is Contrary to Religion:** 'They enjoin what is evil and forbid what is good and close their hands.' That is: their every word is contrary to the religion. What they must encourage, they block; and what they must block, they encourage. Due to their miserliness, they close their hands, while mocking and criticizing those believers who spend in His cause, to discourage them.

**The Real Cause of the Hypocrites' Deprivation:** 'They forgot Allah, so He forgot them.' This explains why they are in this state: because they forgot Allah, and the law of God is that He appoints a devil over those who forget Him, who takes their reins. Then, God forgets them—meaning: He overlooks them and deprives them of facilitation. The facilitation and guidance of Allah are granted only to those who remember Him.

**The True Betrayers:** 'Indeed, the hypocrites - they are the defiantly disobedient.' 'Defiantly disobedient' (*fāsiqūn*) here is used in the sense of covenant-breaking. That is: let no one assume because of their claim that they are nominal Muslims. They are not nominal Muslims, but are the true covenant-breakers and betrayers. They made a covenant of hearing and obeying, and then broke it, attempting to destroy Islam after claiming it. Therefore, they are the leaders in betrayal.

**The Fate of the Betrayers:** 'Allah has promised the hypocrites, both men and women, and the disbelievers the fire of Hell, wherein they will abide eternally.' This is their end: they will receive no favor because of their claim to Islam, but will be cast into Hell eternally alongside the open disbelievers. 'It is sufficient for them' (*hiya hasbuhum*)—meaning: Hell is sufficient to crush them; no other thing is needed. 'And Allah has cursed them, and for them is an enduring punishment'—meaning: Allah has cursed them, closing the doors of hope upon them. An enduring punishment will be theirs, from which they will never escape.

**Direct Threat to the Hypocrites:** '[You are] like those before you; they were stronger than you in power and more abundant in wealth and children. They enjoyed their portion, and you have enjoyed your portion as those before you enjoyed their portion, and you have engaged [in idle talk] like that in which they engaged. Those - their deeds have become worthless in this world and the Hereafter, and it is those who are the losers.' This is a threat addressed to them directly, which makes it more severe. He stated: do not exult in your wealth and children, assuming your path is not disliked by God. Those before you were given more than you, but what happened? When they enjoyed what was decreed for them in this world, God destroyed them. Just so, you have enjoyed your portion and committed the same idle talk and boval-fuzuli; therefore, your destruction has arrived. Just as their deeds went to waste and they failed, your deeds will go to waste and you will fail. The meaning is: look at your deeds, not at your wealth and children; God grants wealth and children to the wicked too; His decisions are based on deeds and character.

**Referencing the Fate of Past Nations:** 'Has there not reached them the news of those before them - the people of Noah and [the tribes of] 'Ad and Thamud and the people of Abraham and the companions [i.e., dwellers] of Madyan and the overturned cities? Their messengers came to them with clear proofs. And Allah would not have wronged them, but they were wronging themselves.' This is the detail of the preceding verse, referencing those nations. Their history was detailed in Al-A'raf. 'The people of Abraham' refers to those whom he invited to monotheism, and then emigrated when they refused. 'The overturned cities' (*al-mu'tafikāt*) refers to the cities of the people of Lot. He stated: these nations were destroyed not because Allah committed any injustice against them, but because they wronged themselves. Allah sent His messengers to complete the proof, but they denied them and persisted in their rebellion, which is why He destroyed them. The meaning is: this history is to repeat with you.

**The Character of the Believers:** Having described the character of the hypocrites (male and female), He described the character of the believers (male and female). Regarding the hypocrites, He stated: they are all of a piece in nurturing hypocrisy. Regarding the believers, He stated: 'The believers, both men and women, are allies of one another.' They are companions and helpers in fulfilling the requirements of faith. When the believing men go out for Jihad, the believing women do not become a chain or a trap for them, but encourage them with sincerity, helping them through their sacrifice, prayers, and loyalty, sharing in the reward. While the hypocrites enjoin evil and forbid good, 'They enjoin what is right and forbid what is evil.' While the hypocrites close their hands and forget God, 'they establish prayer'—which is the greatest means to remember God—'and give zakat'—which opens the hand to spend in His cause and removes miserliness. While the hypocrites are betrayers, 'and obey Allah and His Messenger'—they fulfill their covenant of hearing and obeying with complete uprightness at every stage. 'Those - Allah will have mercy upon them. Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.' Since their dealing with God is different from the hypocrites', God's dealing with them is also different. For the hypocrites, He decreed His curse; for the believers, His mercy. God is Exalted in Might and Wise; His power and wisdom demand that His every dealing be based on justice and mercy. The particle *sa* (soon/will) in *sa-yarhamuhum* is a glad tiding of the near appearance of this mercy.

**Allah's Promise to the Believers:** 'Allah has promised the believers, both men and women, gardens under which rivers flow, wherein they will abide eternally, and pleasant dwellings in gardens of perpetual residence; but approval from Allah is greater. That is the great success.' This stands in direct opposition to verse 68: there, the threat was described; here, the promise to the believers. 'Approval' (*riḍwān*) and 'perpetual residence' (*ʿadn*) were discussed elsewhere. Here, it stands in opposition to the curse decreed for the hypocrites. Just as 'curse' is a comprehensive expression for all punishments and eternal deprivation, 'approval' is a comprehensive expression for all mercies, blessings, and joys. For this reason, He stated: 'that is the great success' (*ḏālika huwa al-fawzu al-ʿaẓīmu*). Keep in mind 'Those - they are the losers' (*ulā'ika humu al-ḫāsirūna*) when reading this.

Commentary on Verses 73-80: Next, He commanded the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): these hypocrites are exploiting your generosity to remain with the Muslims; now, adopt a harsh attitude toward them, so they either reform or face their end. In their gatherings, they utter words of disbelief, while attempting to satisfy you through false oaths. Their designs were highly dangerous, but Allah did not let them succeed. Respite can no longer be granted to them. Allah and His Messenger blessed them from His bounty, but this, instead of gratitude, made them commit mischief and hostility. They claimed that if Allah gave them wealth, they would spend it in His cause; but when He did, they turned away, and this sowed the seeds of hypocrisy deep in their hearts, which can no longer be uprooted. Afterward, He stated: they not only close their hands, but mock those sincere believers who spend in His cause from their hard earning, to discourage them. Along with this, He strictly prohibited the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) from asking forgiveness for them, as they no longer deserve this mercy; they deserve only harshness. Recite the verses:

**Command to Prosecute the Hypocrites Severely:** Although the primary purpose here is to command the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to change his attitude toward the hypocrites and prosecute them severely, He also referenced the disbelievers, indicating that these hypocrites are no longer part of the Muslim community but belong to the disbelievers. *Jihād* encompasses fighting, severe prosecution, and apprehension. The meaning is: 'O Prophet, strive against the disbelievers and the hypocrites'—strive against the disbelievers in the manner commanded under the declaration of disassociation, and prosecute these hypocrites severely. 'and be harsh upon them' (*wa-uġluẓ ʿalayhim*) explains this: they did not appreciate the soft attitude you chose up to now; exploiting your generosity, they became bolder in their mischief. They turned false excuses and false oaths into their shield, while you overlooked them despite knowing their deceit. This can no longer be allowed. Prosecute them severely, and test them on a rigid criterion, so they realize that this cover of lies is gone. They must either become true Muslims or face the fate of the disbelievers. 'and their refuge is Hell, and wretched is the destination'—they deserve harshness in this world, and Hell is their wretched destination, where severe angels are appointed who will show no mercy, but will execute God's command: 'over which are [appointed] angels, harsh and severe; they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them.' (At-Tahrim: 6).

**The Hypocrites' Lies and Grave Disbelief:** 'They swear by Allah that they did not say [anything].' In verse 65 above, it passed that the hypocrites mocked Allah, His verses, and His Messenger in their gatherings, but when questioned, they denied it, swearing: 'We did not say anything; we were only conversing and playing.' He stated: their oaths are false. 'while they had said the word of disbelief'—they utter words of disbelief, and deny it when questioned. Stating this here serves as proof for why they are grouped with the disbelievers in the preceding verse. 'and disbelieved after their Islam' points to the gravity of their crime—namely: they claimed Islam, but committed disbelief through this mockery. It should noted that claiming Islam while committing disbelief is more disliked by Allah than open disbelief, because it adds deceit to disbelief. The Quran pointed to this in Surah As-Saff: 'O you who have believed, why do you say what you do not do? Great is hatred in the sight of Allah that you say what you do not do.' (As-Saff: 2-3). For this reason, the hypocrites, as explicitly stated in the Quran, will be in the lowest depths of the Fire.

**The Hypocrites' Evil Designs and Their Failure:** 'and they planned that which they were not to attain'—these words express in a highly eloquent manner their evil designs and their failures. The meaning is: if they had the power, they would have uprooted Islam, but Allah did not let their designs succeed. Under verse 48, we mentioned some of their mischiefs, and more will be mentioned ahead; but they faced defeat at every turn because Allah exposed their secrets.

**The Lowliness and Ingratitude of the Hypocrites:** 'And they did not resent except that Allah and His Messenger had enriched them of His bounty.' This points to their ingratitude: Allah and His Messenger showed them favor, and they repaid it by mocking Allah and His Messenger and conspiring against Islam. It should be kept in mind that before Islam, the people of Medina were generally poor; after Islam, when the doors of victory opened, everyone's state changed, but the hypocrites' state changed most. First, the Prophet would show them favor to win them over; second, they would take from the state under various excuses; third, they had only taking hands, not giving hands. Whenever a call to spend arose, they avoided it. In this manner, they became rich, and they repaid Islam by conspiring against it.

**Warning to the Hypocrites:** 'So if they repent, it is better for you; but if they turn away, Allah will punish them with a painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter. And there will not be for them on earth any protector or helper.' This is a threat: if they repent, it is better for them; if not, God will punish them in this world and the Hereafter. They will share in the punishment of the disbelievers in this world, and will be with them in the Hereafter, and no protector or helper will be theirs on earth. Those with whom they conspire will face such trouble that they will not be able to help themselves, let alone helping others.

Commentary on Verses 75-77:

'And among them are those who made a covenant with Allah, [saying], "If He should give us of His bounty, we will surely give charity and be among the righteous."' **The Weakness of Those Who Became Rich Through Islam:** This refers to those who became rich through Islam: when they were poor, they expressed their desire: 'If Allah sours our state, we will spend with liberality in His cause and serve the religion to be among the righteous'; but when Allah fulfilled their desire, giving them of His bounty, they turned away as if they had never made any covenant with Allah and His Messenger. 'Turning away' (*tawallaw*) can be of a nature that allows for return, but adding 'while they were averse' (*wa-hum muʿriḍūna*) clarified that they turned away never to look back.

'But when He gave them from His bounty, they were stingy with it and turned away while they were averse.' This is a strange human weakness: when a person lacks something, he desires: 'If I receive it, I will not waste it like others, but will spend it on high targets to establish a model of goodness and justice'; but when Allah gives it to him, he forgets what covenants he made with his Lord and how he expressed his desires; rather, taking it as a result of his own capability, he becomes its owner and uses it for Satan more than for God. Highly few are those who recognize the Giver's right and use the blessing correctly.

'So He penalized them with hypocrisy in their hearts until the Day they will meet Him - because they failed Allah in what they promised Him and because they used to lie.' *A'qaba* means to bring something after another as its result. That is: because of their breach of covenant and continuous lies, God sowed the seeds of hypocrisy deep in their hearts, which will remain there until the Day of Resurrection, to be uprooted only when they face their recompense. The meaning is: they spent a long time nurturing their hypocrisy; this did not enter them suddenly, but was the result of deliberate breach of covenant and continuous lies, which will never leave them. Do not expect them to repent and reform. Their veil will be removed only when they see the reality. A similar statement is made ahead regarding the builders of the Masjid al-Dirar: 'The building which they built will not cease to be [a cause of] doubt in their hearts until their hearts are cut [to pieces]. And Allah is Knowing and Wise.' (At-Tawbah: 110).

Commentary on Verses 78-79:

'Do they not know that Allah knows their secrets and their private conversations and that Allah is the Knower of the unseen?' **The Dull-Wittedness of the Hypocrites:** 'Do they not know that Allah...' is an expression of wonder and grief. The meaning is: after all this teaching, purification, and exposure of realities, are they still so dull-witted as not to understand that God knows their secrets and private conversations, and is Knower of the unseen? We explained elsewhere that God knows everything through His actions and His attributes; therefore, here and in other places, His knowledge is expressed through both the verb and the noun. From the perspective of structure, this verse serves as an introduction to the subsequent verse, which exposes those private conversations and criticisms they carried out in their circles to discourage the believers.

'Those who criticize the contributors among the believers concerning [their] charities and those who find nothing except their effort so they mock them - Allah will mock them, and for them is a painful punishment.' 'Those' (*al-laḏīna*) is, in my view, an appositive for 'their private conversations' (*naǧwāhum*). While I am not completely certain of this, I have preserved it in the translation. Al-Kashshaf also points to this. *Mutatāwi'* or *mutāwi'* refers to the one who does not confine himself to obligatorily required deeds, but voluntarily performs extra deeds. *Lamz* means to criticize, mock, or blame.

Above, He stated that the hypocrites avoid spending in the cause of Allah. Now, He stated: they not only avoid it, but cannot tolerate seeing others spend. Whomever they see spend, they make the target of their criticism: those believers who spend with liberality, they criticize as being showy and desiring fame; and those poor ones who find nothing except their hard earning, spending it in His cause, they mock—claiming that they are trying to erase Hatim's name from the world.

This psychological aspect of misers was discussed elsewhere: to cover their miserliness, they attempt to have others remain misers as well. The noseless wants everyone to be noseless so no one can call him noseless. This was the psychology of the hypocrites. Furthermore, it satisfied their hostility toward Islam. They did not wish to spend for Islam, nor did they like seeing others spend. When they saw others spend with liberality—as if filling their own homes—and saw a poor laborer spend from his hard earning with joy—as if buying the kingdom of the two worlds for a handful of dates—they boiled with anger and burned with jealousy. Then, they vented their anger through criticism, mockery, and taunts.

**Allah's Respite:** 'Allah will mock them' is similar to 'Allah mocks them' in Al-Baqarah—meaning: they mock the believers, but Allah mocks them by extending their rope so they commit more mischief, and then He will apprehend them from where they do not expect.

**Strictness Regarding the Hypocrites and Prohibiting Asking Forgiveness for Them:**

اِسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ اَوْ لَا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ ۭاِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِيْنَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَّغْفِرَ اللّٰهُ لَهُمْ ۭ ذٰلِكَ بِاَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوْا بِاللّٰهِ وَرَسُوْلِهٖ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفٰسِقِيْنَ

This is based on the instruction 'be harsh upon them' above. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was mercy and concern embodied; therefore, despite the hypocrites' mischiefs, he desired their reform, asking forgiveness for them just as he did for the entire Ummah. But their wretchedness reached the point where they could not tolerate others doing good for Islam, making it their profession to discourage every well-wisher. Thus, Allah prohibited you from asking forgiveness for these wretched ones, and with such finality: 'even if you ask forgiveness for them seventy times - never will Allah forgive them.' Obviously, 'seventy' (*sab'īn*) does not denote a count, but abundance. This usage is common in both Arabic and Urdu.

**Declaration of the Hypocrites' Disbelief:** 'That is because they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger.' This explains why they are deprived of forgiveness: because they are deniers of Allah and His Messenger. This is a declaration of the disbelief of those who claimed faith and performed prayer, but Allah did not accept their faith because of their deeds. 'And Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people'—'guidance' is used in the sense of guidance to the destination. We explained this elsewhere. 'Defiantly disobedient' (*fāsiqūn*) here is used in the sense of covenant-breaking. See what we wrote under verse 74.

Commentary on Verses 81-89: These verses were revealed during the Battle of Tabuk regarding those hypocrites who remained at home under false excuses and were pleased with their deceit. The context is completely clear.

فَرِحَ الْمُخَلَّفُوْنَ بِمَقْعَدِهِمْ خِلٰفَ رَسُوْلِ اللّٰهِ وَكَرِهُوْٓا اَنْ يُّجَاهِدُوْا بِاَمْوَالِهِمْ وَاَنْفُسِهِمْ فِيْ سَبِيْلِ اللّٰهِ وَقَالُوْا لَا تَنْفِرُوْا فِي الْحَرِّ قُلْ نَارُ جَهَنَّمَ اَشَدُّ حَرًّا لَوْ كَانُوْا يَفْقَهُوْنَ 

**The Meaning of 'al-Mukhallafun':** *Mukhallaf* means the one who was left behind. By *mukhallafūn* here, what is meant is those who avoided Tabuk under false excuses. By using this word, the Quran clarified their status: they assume they received permission, but in reality, they are those left behind and ignored. Knowing their inner state, the Prophet accepted their excuses because such cowards are better left at home; if they had joined, they would have caused mischief.

**The Meaning of 'Khilaf':** *K͟hilāf* is used in the Quran in two meanings: first, contrary or alternating—as in 'or that their hands and feet be cut off from opposite sides' (Al-Ma'idah); second, after or behind—as in 'then they would not have remained after you except for a little.' (Al-Isra: 76). Here, it carries this second meaning. That is: they are pleased that the Messenger of Allah left with his devoted companions while they remained at home; but this is no source of joy, but is proof of their ruin. The sheep left behind falls prey to the wolf. 'and disliked to strive with their wealth and their lives' is the contrary of 'rejoiced'. What was a source of joy was disliked by them, and what was a source of grief made them rejoice. 'and said, "Do not go forth in the heat."' They not only remained at home, but discouraged others, warning them of the hot season. It should be kept in mind that Tabuk took place in a highly hot season. 'Say, "The fire of Hell is more intense in heat" - if they would but understand.' Tell them: for those who choose ease, the fire of Hell is ahead, which is far hotter than this heat. They fled from this heat to plunge into the fire of Hell. If only they could understand.

فَلْيَضْحَكُوْا قَلِيْلًا وَّلْيَبْكُوْا كَثِيْرًا جَزَاءً بِمَا كَانُوْا يَكْسِبُوْنَ 

**The Action and Recompense Together:** Since they fled from this heat to plunge into the fire of Hell, they must laugh little and weep much in recompense for what they committed; but as passed in the preceding verse, they are devoid of understanding, which is why they are pleased with their ruin. Generally, commentators have taken this as a report, but in our view, this is not correct. Here, the action and recompense are placed before the eyes, because the one who possesses insight sees his recompense in his action in this world, whereas the blind remain deprived of it.

فَاِنْ رَّجَعَكَ اللّٰهُ اِلٰى طَاۗىِٕفَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ فَاسْتَاْذَنُوْكَ لِلْخُرُوْجِ فَقُلْ لَّنْ تَخْرُجُوْا مَعِيَ اَبَدًا وَّلَنْ تُقَاتِلُوْا مَعِيَ عَدُوًّا اِنَّكُمْ رَضِيْتُمْ بِالْقُعُوْدِ اَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ فَاقْعُدُوْا مَعَ الْخٰلِفِيْن 

*Raǧa'a* is used as both transitive and intransitive.

**The Context and Indications of These Verses:** The style of 'If Allah returns you...' (*fa-in raǧaʿaka Allāhu*) shows that these verses were revealed during the journey of Tabuk, pointing to his return from this journey. Second, it shows that because of their mischiefs, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) became so averse to them that he did not wish to see their faces. This aversion was in accordance with the will and command of Allah. Therefore, He instructed: if Allah returns you to a group of them, and they seek permission to join any future battle to clear their shame and prove their loyalty, reject their request. This is one aspect of 'be harsh upon them' commanded in verse 74. Third, it points to their assumption that because of the Roman army, the Muslims would not return to Medina. They harbored a similar assumption during Hudaybiyyah, as mentioned in Surah Al-Fath: 'But you thought that the Messenger and the believers would never return to their families...' (Al-Fath: 12).

'Say, "Never will you go forth with me, ever, and never will you fight with me against an enemy. Indeed, you were satisfied with sitting [at home] the first time, so sit with those who stay behind."' **Depriving the Hypocrites of Collective Service:** Tell them clearly: you will never be my companions in any journey, nor fight with me against any enemy. Go and sit with those who remain at home. This commanded the Prophet to bar them from any collective service, so they would be disgraced and their cover of excuses ended. Up to now, they sought permission to avoid Jihad; now, the Prophet was instructed: if they seek permission to join, do not grant it to them. A similar warning passed in Surah Al-Fath, verse 15. In our view, the verse of Al-Fath relates to the negation of facilitation, while this relates to prohibition.

وَلَا تُصَلِّ عَلٰٓي اَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ مَّاتَ اَبَدًا وَّلَا تَقُمْ عَلٰي قَبْرِهٖ اِنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوْا بِاللّٰهِ وَرَسُوْلِهٖ وَمَاتُوْا وَهُمْ فٰسِقُوْنَ 

**Prohibiting the Funeral Prayer and Asking Forgiveness for the Hypocrites:** This is a harsher command to separate them from the community. In verse 80, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was prohibited from asking forgiveness for them; now, He prohibited performing the funeral prayer for them and standing at their graves to ask forgiveness, closing any connection with them in life and death. The final connection of a person with the community is to be buried by his brothers and accompanied by their prayers; this prohibition cut this final connection. 'Indeed, they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger and died while they were defiantly disobedient' explains the reason: they died in disbelief. 'Defiantly disobedient' (*fāsiqūn*) here is used in the sense of breach of covenant and treachery. Although they claimed faith and performed certain rites, they broke their covenant, as proven by their deeds.

وَلَا تُعْجِبْكَ اَمْوَالُهُمْ وَاَوْلَادُهُمْ ۭ اِنَّمَا يُرِيْدُ اللّٰهُ اَنْ يُّعَذِّبَهُمْ بِهَا فِي الْاٰخِرَةِ وَتَزْهَقَ اَنْفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كٰفِرُوْنَ

This was explained in verse 55 of this Surah.

Commentary on Verses 86-87:

'And when a surah was revealed [enjoining], "Believe in Allah and strive with His Messenger,"' The word 'surah' (*sūrah*) here is not used in its technical sense, but refers to some part or portion of the Quran. 'Believe in Allah and strive with His Messenger'—the verb 'believe' (*āminū*) is used in its true sense—namely: prove your faith by going out for Jihad with the Prophet. 'those of wealth among them asked your permission and said, "Leave us; let us be with those who sit."' *Tawl* means power and wealth. That is: when the call to Jihad arises, they prove false. Their poor have no say; but even those possessing tanners, weapons, and means seek permission to remain at home. 'and said, "Leave us; let us be with those who sit"'—in my view, this reports their inner intent through their words. Sometimes, the Quran reports what people harbor in their hearts through their words. For example, regarding the Jews, He stated: 'and they said, "We hear and we disobey."' Although they said 'we hear and we obey,' their heart's voice and their deeds proved they said 'we hear and we disobey.' Just so, although these hypocrites presented excuses, their inner desire was to remain with those who stay behind out of cowardice and love for ease. The Quran reported this inner desire through their words, which contains a highly subtle sarcasm, which becomes even sharper in the subsequent verse.

رَضُوْا بِاَنْ يَّكُوْنُوْا مَعَ الْخَوَالِفِ وَطُبِعَ عَلٰي قُلُوْبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَفْقَهُوْنَ 

**Choosing to Be with Women:** *K͟hawālif* refers to women, because men go out for war and other tasks, while women remain at home to care for children and houses. He stated: being men, they chose to remain at home like women. This moral and faithful death resulted in sealing their hearts, so they no longer understand what end they are heading to.

Commentary on Verses 88-89:

**The Character of the True Believers:** 'But the Messenger and those who believed with him strove with their wealth and their lives, and those - for them are good things, and it is those who are the successful. Allah has prepared for them gardens under which rivers flow, wherein they will abide eternally. That is the great success.' This describes the character and end of the true believers, praising them while appealing to the honor of the hypocrites: the Prophet's true companions do not run with excuses when Jihad is commanded, but present themselves with their wealth and lives. He stated: true success belongs to them.

The upcoming subject (Verses 90 to 99): Above, the discussion was regarding the urban hypocrites living in and around Medina. Now, He mentioned those living in the desert. Their diseases are of the same nature; therefore, their ruling is also the same. In this connection, He described the true unable ones so no confusion would occur between the excuse-makers and the true unable ones. Since this is the final discussion on the hypocrites, and there was a danger that some innocent person might be affected, drawing a clear line between the two was necessary. Recite the verses:

وَجَاۗءَ الْمُعَذِّرُوْنَ مِنَ الْاَعْرَابِ لِيُؤْذَنَ لَهُمْ وَقَعَدَ الَّذِيْنَ كَذَبُوا اللّٰهَ وَرَسُوْلَهٗ ۭسَيُصِيْبُ الَّذِيْنَ كَفَرُوْا مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ اَلِيْم 

*Mu'ad͟h-d͟hir* means the one who invents false and baseless excuses to avoid any responsibility. *A'rab* is the plural of *A'rabiy*, which refers to the desert-dwelling Arabs. Here, it refers to those bards living around Medina who embraced Islam out of fear of its growing power, though Islam had not entered their hearts. First, they came to Islam because of its power rather than its spiritual attraction. Second, due to being far from the center, teaching them was difficult, and they did not wish to leave their business and herds to benefit from the Prophet's company. Those among them who possessed capacity could have made an effort to learn, but the majority were ignorant and unlettered. These were the ones who fanned the fire of apostasy after the Prophet's passing, which Hazrat Abu Bakr Al-Siddiq had to crush with full effort.

'And those who had lied to Allah and His Messenger sat [at home].' That is: some came to present false excuses, and some, being false in their claim, did not even bother to present any excuse, remaining at home. 'There will strike those who disbelieved among them a painful punishment'—'among them' (*minhum*) refers to the desert-dwellers. Since there was a group of sincere believers among them, who were standard-bearers of sacrifice, He stated: those who chose disbelief—remaining at home under false excuses or ignoring the command—will face a painful punishment, and only those who kept their covenant or were truly unable are excluded.

Commentary on Verses 91-93:

This describes the difference between the excuse-makers and the true unable ones, so no excuse-maker can claim excuse and no true unable one is accused of hypocrisy.

'There is not upon the weak or upon the ill or upon those who do not find anything to spend any discomfort when they are sincere to Allah and His Messenger. There is not against the doers of good any cause [for blame]. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.' That is: the weak, the ill, and the poor who cannot manage their provisions and mounts for Jihad are of the true unable ones. It is no sin if they do not join, on the condition that they remain sincere to Allah and His Messenger. This condition is highly important, because some ill or poor people might remain at home while conspiring to harm the Muslims. Such people will not be spared. Only those who, being unable to join, do whatever they can to serve Islam and pray for the victory of the Muslims will be spared. He stated: such people are the doers of good (*muhsinīn*), and there is no blame upon them.

'Nor [is there blame] upon those who, when they came to you that you might give them mounts, you said, "I do not find anything for you to ride upon." They turned back while their eyes overflowed with tears of grief that they did not find anything to spend.' Although their ruling was included in the preceding verse, He mentioned them separately to show that those whose obstacle is poverty—not weakness of faith—display their sincere desire. Some people are pleased to find that they are poor, and therefore free of responsibility; but these people do not accept being deprived of this blessing easily, attempting to find any mount, and when they fail, their hearts are grieved and their eyes overflow with tears. Thus, poverty is a true excuse for the poor only when their every nerve bears witness to their desire.

'The cause [for blame] is only against those who ask permission of you while they are rich. They are satisfied to be with those who stay behind, and Allah has sealed their hearts so they do not know.' The true excuse-makers are those who possess wealth, weapons, and mounts, yet seek permission to avoid Jihad. Their lack of honor reached the point where they chose to remain at home like women, and Allah has sealed their hearts so they do not know what end they are heading to.

يَعْتَذِرُوْنَ اِلَيْكُمْ اِذَا رَجَعْتُمْ اِلَيْهِمْ ۭ قُلْ لَّا تَعْتَذِرُوْا لَنْ نُّؤْمِنَ لَكُمْ قَدْ نَبَّاَنَا اللّٰهُ مِنْ اَخْبَارِكُمْ ۭ وَسَيَرَى اللّٰهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُوْلُهٗ ثُمَّ تُرَدُّوْنَ اِلٰى عٰلِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهَادَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُوْنَ 

*I'tid͟hār* means inventing excuses and making up stories. That is: when you return from this journey (Tabuk), these hypocrites will present their stories to satisfy you (the address is to the Muslims). 'Say, "Make no excuse; we will not believe you. Allah has already informed us of your news."' 'Say' (*qul*) is singular, while the subsequent verbs are plural. This shows that this is the answer given to the hypocrites on behalf of all Muslims through the Prophet's tongue. The Prophet's tongue represents the hearts of all sincere believers. This also reveals the mutual trust, good expectation, and complete harmony between the Prophet and the community, while highlighting the sarcasm against the hypocrites. 'we will not believe you; Allah has already informed us of your news'—meaning: since Allah has informed us of your state, we will not listen to your stories; make no excuse.

**Judging Based on Outward Conditions:** This shows that the reason why their excuses were rejected was that Allah—the Knower of the unseen—had exposed them. Aside from the Prophet, since no one has access to this divine knowledge, they will judge in such matters solely based on outward conditions and Shariah proofs. If there is no proof to reject someone's excuse, it must be accepted, even if it is false. We will accept everyone who claims Islam, unless we have a Shariah proof to reject their claim—even if they are hypocrites in their hearts.

'And Allah will observe your activity, and [so will] His Messenger; then you will be returned to the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, and He will inform you of what you used to do.' That is: instead of inventing excuses, prove your uprightness through your deeds, for Allah and His Messenger will observe your activity. These stories will not benefit you; and remember that you must return to the Knower of the unseen, who will place your deeds before you, showing the sincerity or malice in them.

سَيَحْلِفُوْنَ بِاللّٰهِ لَكُمْ اِذَا انْقَلَبْتُمْ اِلَيْهِمْ لِتُعْرِضُوْا عَنْهُمْ ۭ فَاَعْرِضُوْا عَنْهُمْ ۭ اِنَّهُمْ رِجْسٌ ۡ وَمَاْوٰىهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۚ جَزَاءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوْا يَكْسِبُوْنَ
