In this verse, the word 'turning away' (*iʿrāḍ*) is used in two different meanings: first, turning away from someone; second, accepting someone's excuse to overlook and be pleased with them. He stated: upon your return, they will swear to you to satisfy you, so you overlook them; so turn away from them, for they are impure (*riǧs*), and turning away from impurity is best. The refuge of this rubbish is Hell.

يَحْلِفُوْنَ لَكُمْ لِتَرْضَوْا عَنْهُمْ ۚ فَاِنْ تَرْضَوْا عَنْهُمْ فَاِنَّ اللّٰهَ لَا يَرْضٰى عَنِ الْقَوْمِ الْفٰسِقِيْنَ 

'They swear to you that you might be pleased with them. But if you should be pleased with them - indeed, Allah is not pleased with the defiantly disobedient people.' Swearing oaths is meant to satisfy you. If you are pleased with them, remember that Allah is not pleased with these covenant-breakers. Allah will be pleased only when they reform their path through repentance.

اَلْاَعْرَابُ اَشَدُّ كُفْرًا وَّنِفَاقًا وَّاَجْدَرُ اَلَّا يَعْلَمُوْا حُدُوْدَ مَآ اَنْزَلَ اللّٰهَ عَلٰي رَسُوْلِهٖ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ عَلِيْمٌ حَكِيْمٌ 

**The Cause of God's Anger is Their Disbelief and Hypocrisy:** This explains why they deserve His anger, so that even if the Muslims are pleased with them, Allah will not be pleased. He stated: they deserve no favor. They are more intense in disbelief and hypocrisy. They did not benefit from the Prophet's company, nor did they attempt to learn His Shariah. Consequently, the disease of disbelief and hypocrisy seized them. If they valued faith, they would have maintained connection with the Prophet to learn, but they excused themselves: 'our wealth and families kept us busy.' Ahead, their indifference is mentioned:

'And it is not for the believers to go forth [to battle] all at once. For there should separate from every division of them a group to obtain understanding in the religion and warn their people when they return to them that they might be cautious.' (At-Tawbah: 122).

The meaning is: their being desert-dwellers is no excuse. They were supposed to rise to learn, but they remained in their disbelief; therefore, they deserved the ignorance and hypocrisy they nurtured. Allah is Knowing and Wise. His dealing with everyone is based on His knowledge and wisdom. He does not impose His religion on anyone, but grants it to those who prove themselves worthy.

وَمِنَ الْاَعْرَابِ مَنْ يَّتَّخِذُ مَا يُنْفِقُ مَغْرَمًا وَّيَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمُ الدَّوَاۗىِٕءَ ۭ عَلَيْهِمْ دَاۗىِٕرَةُ السَّوْءِ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ سَمِيْعٌ عَلِيْمٌ 

That is: their hostility toward Islam is such that they do not spend in His cause, and if they are forced to, they consider it a fine (*maġram*). They constantly wait for a turn of fortune to strike you; 'upon them is a misfortune of evil'—meaning: they wait for misfortune to strike you, but misfortune is upon them, and an evil misfortune at that. Allah is Hearing and Knowing, and His dealing with them is based on His hearing and knowing.

وَمِنَ الْاَعْرَابِ مَنْ يُّؤْمِنُ بِاللّٰهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْاٰخِرِ وَيَتَّخِذُ مَا يُنْفِقُ قُرُبٰتٍ عِنْدَ اللّٰهِ وَصَلَوٰتِ الرَّسُوْلِ ۭ اَلَآ اِنَّهَا قُرْبَةٌ لَّهُمْ ۭ سَـيُدْخِلُهُمُ اللّٰهُ فِيْ رَحْمَتِهٖ ۭ اِنَّ اللّٰهَ غَفُوْرٌ رَّحِيْمٌ 

*Qurubāt* is the plural of *qurbah*, which refers to those deeds of goodness and spending that serve as a means of nearness to God.

**The Sincere and Upright Among the Desert-Dwellers:** This describes those who were sincere. He stated: among them are true believers who believe in Allah and the Last Day. Unlike the hypocrites, they do not consider spending as a fine, but make their spending and the Prophet's prayers a means of nearness to God. In verse 80, the Prophet was prohibited from asking forgiveness for the hypocrites, and in verse 84, from performing their funeral prayer; but the sincere enjoyed both. Thus, He stated: they make their spending and the Prophet's prayers a means of nearness, whereas the hypocrites are deprived of both. 'Unquestionably, it is a means of nearness for them' (*alā innahā qurbatun lahum*)—the singular feminine pronoun returns to both things. This style was explained elsewhere.

Generally, commentators have taken this as meaning: they make their spending a means to obtain the Prophet's prayers; but I hesitate to accept this. This requires assuming omitted words where no context exists. Maulana Farahi's view is the same as I chose, which aligns with both the grammar and the context.

The upcoming subject (Verses 100 to 112): Next, He praised those who were foremost in Islam, or followed them with sincerity. These represent the true core of the Islamic society. Allah promised them His approval and success. Then, He pointed to the hypocrites of Medina and the desert, warning them of punishment in this world, the grave, and the Hereafter—which shows that they have no connection to the Islamic society. Then, He mentioned those who repented with sincerity, giving them glad tidings of acceptance, while giving several instructions to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) regarding their reform. Also, regarding some, He instructed to postpone their decision. Afterward, He mentioned the Masjid al-Dirar and its builders, announcing His decision. Ahead, He referred to that covenant between Allah and the believers, explaining the attributes the believers must possess, so the society would be purified of all corrupt elements. Recite the verses:

وَالسّٰبِقُوْنَ الْاَوَّلُوْنَ مِنَ الْمُهٰجِرِيْنَ وَالْاَنْصَارِ وَالَّذِيْنَ اتَّبَعُوْهُمْ بِاِحْسَانٍ ۙ رَّضِيَ اللّٰهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوْا عَنْهُ وَاَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَنّٰتٍ تَجْرِيْ تَحْتَهَا الْاَنْهٰرُ خٰلِدِيْنَ فِيْهَآ اَبَدًا ۭ ذٰلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيْمُ 

**The Best Elements of the Islamic Society:** Now, He described who the best elements and the true asset of the Islamic society are. This is meant to place before the common Muslims those whose deeds serve as a model for them, and to show the hypocrites that they cannot remain in this community unless they adopt their character. He stated: the vanguard of this Ummah consists of those foremost among the Muhajirun and Ansar, who accepted the call when doing so required facing all enmities, and who defended the Prophet when doing so was equivalent to fighting everyone.

**The Second Class of the Islamic Society:** In the second class are those who followed the foremost ones with sincerity (*bi-ihsān*), without any show, interest, hesitation, or hypocrisy. Having taken the step, they never looked back; they cut their past ties completely, and joined the believers so as to become one with them. This sincerity and alignment of outward and inward is expressed through 'sincerity' (*ihsān*), which also means performing a task with excellence.

**The Great Glad Tiding of Approval:** 'Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him' is a great glad tiding and the highest praise. He stated: Allah is pleased with them, because they fulfilled His requirements of patience, resolve, and sacrifice, keeping their covenant through all enmities; and they are pleased with Allah, because they accepted His every dealing as based on His justice, wisdom, and mercy. If a calamity struck them, they took it as a cure; if difficulty arose, they took it as a test; if ease came, they showed gratitude; if grief came, they showed patience. Nothing could shake them; they remained firm, receiving the glad tiding: 'O reassured soul, return to your Lord, well-pleased and pleasing [to Him].' 'and He has prepared for them gardens under which rivers flow, wherein they will abide eternally, forever. That is the great success.' This is their recompense. He stated: the true success is this, and let whoever wants to play the game play it for this. Losing life for this temporary world is equivalent to throwing oneself into eternal ruin.

وَمِمَّنْ حَوْلَكُمْ مِّنَ الْاَعْرَابِ مُنٰفِقُوْنَ ړ وَمِنْ اَھْلِ الْمَدِيْنَةِ ڀ مَرَدُوْا عَلَي النِّفَاقِ ۣ لَا تَعْمَلُهُمْ ۭ نَحْنُ نَعْلَمُهُمْ ۭ سَنُعَذِّبُهُمْ مَّرَّتَيْنِ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّوْنَ اِلٰي عَذَابٍ عَظِيْمٍ 

**Pointing to the Hypocrites:** He stated: among the desert-dwellers are hypocrites, and among the people of Medina is a group of hypocrites who remain with you for their interests, and are so experienced in hypocrisy that you cannot recognize them; Allah knows them. *Maradū ʿalā al-nifāq* means: they are highly skilled and puktah-kar in hypocrisy, displaying Islam so well that they easily deceive the Muslims. 'you do not know them, [but] We know them' is a warning to both: warning the Muslims to remain alert, and warning the hypocrites that though others may be deceived, Allah knows them and will punish them. 'We will punish them twice' points to the punishment they will face at the hands of the Muslims, and the punishment in the grave. 'then they will be returned to a great punishment' points to the punishment of the Hereafter, which is the most severe. Above, the great success was promised to the believers; here, this great punishment is promised to the hypocrites.

Commentary on Verses 102-105:

'And [there are] others who have acknowledged their sins. They had mixed a righteous deed with another that was bad. Perhaps Allah will turn to them in forgiveness. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.' This refers to those who had committed errors at Tabuk, but some faith remained within them. When this Surah shook the hypocrites, their eyes opened: instead of inventing excuses, they confessed their sins with sincerity, placing themselves before Allah and His Messenger. Narrations show that some went to the extent of tying themselves to the pillars of the Prophet's Mosque, swearing not to eat, drink, or leave until they received forgiveness. Sometimes, a servant's sincerity and regret over his sins is highly beloved to Allah; thus, He accepted their confession, promising them forgiveness in 'Perhaps Allah will turn to them in forgiveness.' Reflect on how the words of the Quran affected the hearts of those people, and how our hearts are today! The Quran is the same, and hypocrisy exists among us, but our hearts do not choose to lose sleep over our sins. Today, people will fast to obtain a rise in salary, but few will lose sleep over their sins. 'They had mixed a righteous deed with another that was bad' explains why they deserved favor: they did not nurture hypocrisy, but performed good deeds alongside bad ones; when they slipped, they rose and reformed. This is why they deserved His mercy. 'Perhaps Allah will turn to them in forgiveness. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful' is the glad tiding of forgiveness, but it is expressed through *'asā* (perhaps) to show that it is conditional, as He clarified in 'And say, "Do [as you will], for Allah will see your work, and [so will] His Messenger and the believers."'—meaning: prove your sincerity through your deeds, for Allah, His Messenger, and the believers will observe your activity, and your final decision depends on this; which is like a warning to them to perform deeds in accordance with His pleasure.

'Take, [O Muhammad], from their wealth a charity by which you purify them and cause them increase, and invoke [Allah's blessings] upon them. Indeed, your invocations are reassurance for them. And Allah is Hearing and Knowing.' Above in verses 53-54, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was instructed not to accept the spending of the hypocrites; indeed, you would reject some; and in verse 84, you were prohibited from performing their funeral prayer. But for those who repented, these doors of mercy were opened. He stated: accept their charities, for through them you will purify them of ruder-stuff and beautify them with virtues, and invoke blessings upon them, for your invocation is reassurance for them.

**Difference Between 'Tathir' (Purifying) and 'Tazkiyah' (Increase):** In the usage of the Quran, *tathīr* relates to purifying from outward and inward impurities and ruder-stuff, while *tazkiyah* includes both purifying and causing the capacities and virtues to grow.

**An Effective Cure for Hypocrisy:** This shows that the most effective cure for hypocrisy is spending in the cause of Allah. This disease arises from the love of the world, which is the source of all ruder-stuff. Spending cuts this root, enabling virtues to grow.

**The Benefit of Spending Belongs to the Spender:** Those who spend do not show favor to Allah and His Messenger, but Allah and His Messenger show favor by accepting it, because they are the ones in need of this purification, not Allah and His Messenger.

**Further Concession to Those Who Repented:** 'and invoke [Allah's blessings] upon them' includes performing the funeral prayer; thus, the prohibition in verse 84 was lifted for those who repented.

'And Allah is Hearing and Knowing' contains both consolation and warning.

'Do they not know that it is Allah who accepts repentance from His servants and receives charities and that Allah is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful?' The preceding verse addressed the Prophet; this verse encourages those who repented to remain active in repentance and spending, for Allah accepts repentance and charity, and He is the Merciful; let those who seek His pleasure perform these deeds. This also shows that repentance requires consistency.

'And say, "Do [as you will], for Allah will see your work, and [so will] His Messenger and the believers. And you will be returned to the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, and He will inform you of what you used to do."' This is a warning to them: do not remain negligent after receiving forgiveness, but prove your sincerity through your deeds; Allah, His Messenger, and the believers will observe your activity. If you attempt to hide anything, remember that you must return to the Knower of the unseen, who will place your deeds before you.

'And [there are] others deferred for the command of Allah - whether He will punish them or whether He will forgive them. And Allah is Knowing and Wise.' *Irǧā'* means to defer or postpone.

Although some had confessed their sins with sincerity, they did not receive immediate forgiveness; rather, their decision was postponed. Ahead in verse 118, reference is made to them, showing that they were three. Narrations identify them as Ka'b bin Malik, Hilal bin Umayyah, and Murarah bin Rabi'. Although they were not hypocrites, having made sacrifices for Islam in past battles, they committed an error at Tabuk, and for certain reasons, they faced a harsher check than others. He stated: their decision is deferred; if He wills, He will punish them; and if He wills, He will forgive them; Allah is Knowing and Wise.

Why did they face such a harsh check despite their past services? Several reasons can exist. First, a check is in proportion to the trust and expectation. An error from them was contrary to their past record, hurting the Prophet's trust, which is a believer's greatest asset. Second, relying on their past services, they perhaps did not feel the gravity of their error as they were supposed to; see the reference in the upcoming verse. A lack of regret is contrary to the spirit of repentance; hence, His wisdom demanded that their forgiveness be postponed until they felt that brokenness of heart which attracts His mercy. Third, since this stage aimed to strike at the roots of hypocrisy, Allah chose to make the Muslims highly sensitive against it. Apprehending such prominent people served this purpose best: if they faced such a check, others would realize that no one is safe. This also taught the Muslims the standard of faith required of everyone.

Commentary on Verses 107-108:

'And [there are] those who took a mosque for harm and disbelief and division among the believers and as a station for whoever had warred against Allah and His Messenger before. And they will surely swear, "We intended only the best." And Allah testifies that indeed they are liars.'

This refers to the most wicked group of the hypocrites. Their mischief was that although Medina had two mosques—Masjid Quba in the suburbs, and Masjid al-Nabawi in the city—they constructed an alternating mosque for their corrupt purposes. Their purpose was to establish an office to plot against Islam, wrapping it in a religious cover so they could boast of their piety while executing their plots. To make it acceptable and respected, they attempted to have the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) perform prayer in it once, but the Prophet postponed it, and when these verses were revealed during his return from Tabuk, he ordered it to be demolished.

**The Corrupt Purposes of Masjid al-Dirar:** The Quran exposed the corrupt purposes of its construction: first, it was constructed 'for harm' (*dirāran*)—to harm Islam and the Muslims. A mosque is a center for prayer and establishing the religion, but this was designed as a net to entrap the Muslims and inject the poison of hypocrisy; second, 'for disbelief' (*kufran*)—to protect and strengthen the disbelief in their hearts; third, 'division among the believers' (*tafrīqan bayna al-mu'minīn*)—to divide the Muslims. A mosque is meant to integrate the Muslims; they wanted to draw people to it and then cause division; fourth, 'as a station for whoever had warred against Allah and His Messenger before'—to provide a refuge for those who fought Allah and His Messenger. The hypocrites' sympathies were with the enemies of Islam, and they wanted to establish an office under the cover of a mosque to serve them.

'And they will surely swear, "We intended only the best." And Allah testifies that indeed they are liars'—they will swear that their purpose was solely the well-being of Islam: to provide a mosque for those who cannot reach Masjid Quba in cold or rainy nights. He stated: they will swear, but Allah testifies that they are liars. *Wa-Allāhu yašhadu* (and Allah testifies) is used as an oath.

Regarding the grammar, some consider 'And [there are] those who...' coordinate with the preceding hypocrites, but in our view, the news (*ḫabar*) is omitted here. In moments of severe anger, the news is often omitted, and the severity of the tone stands in its place. Parallels will come in the final Surahs.

'Do not stand [for prayer] within it, ever. A mosque founded on righteousness from the first day is more worthy for you to stand in. Within it are men who love to purify themselves; and Allah loves those who purify themselves.' The hypocrites constructed this mosque, but its success depended on having the Prophet perform prayer in it, which they failed to achieve. He was prohibited from standing in it, and the tone of this prohibition is directed at the hypocrites.

**Praise of Masjid Quba and its Congregants:** 'A mosque founded on righteousness...' refers to Masjid Quba, as Masjid al-Dirar was constructed to counter it. Allah praised it and its congregants: the mosque founded on piety since the first day is more worthy of your standing, not the one constructed for harm, disbelief, division, and plots. He praised its congregants as loving purification, and Allah loves those who purify themselves. This subtly points to the impurity of the builders of Masjid al-Dirar: since they are disliked by Allah, why should you have any connection with them and their mosque?

Using 'more worthy' (*ahaqqu*) does not imply a comparison where both are worthy; rather, *af'al* is sometimes used in Arabic without denoting comparison. Parallels exist.

**A Mosque is Founded on Piety:** This verse clarifies that a mosque is founded on the piety of its builders' hearts, not on the earth. If the builders' hearts possess piety, it is a mosque; if they possess evil and mischief, it is a temple of idols which, as described ahead, will fall into Hell.

اَفَمَنْ اَسَّسَ بُنْيَانَهٗ عَلٰي تَقْوٰى مِنَ اللّٰهِ وَرِضْوَانٍ خَيْرٌ اَمْ مَّنْ اَسَّسَ بُنْيَانَهٗ عَلٰي شَفَا جُرُفٍ هَارٍ فَانْهَارَ بِهٖ فِيْ نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ ۭ وَاللّٰهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظّٰلِمِيْنَ 

*Šafā* means the edge of something. *Ǧuruf* refers to a hollow bank of a river where water has washed away the soil below. *Hār* means crumbling or falling. This is a metaphor for those who base their deeds on corrupt purposes instead of piety and His pleasure. He stated: they are like the one who constructs his building on a crumbling, hollow bank of a river which falls into Hell, taking him with it. Success belongs only to the deed performed for His pleasure and in accordance with His commands. 'And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people'—'guidance' is used in the sense of guidance to the destination; meaning: Allah will not make such wrongdoers successful; they will fail.

لَا يَزَالُ بُنْيَانُهُمُ الَّذِيْ بَنَوْا رِيْبَةً فِيْ قُلُوْبِهِمْ اِلَّآ اَنْ تَقَطَّعَ قُلُوْبُهُمْ وَاللّٰهُ عَلِيْمٌ حَكِيْمٌ 

That is: by constructing this named mosque, the hypocrites sowed the seeds of hypocrisy so deep in their hearts that it will never leave them until their hearts are cut to pieces—which is a way of expressing an impossibility. This shows that constructing a trial like Masjid al-Dirar is a deed whose evil effects can never be removed; it enters every nerve. Similar is stated ahead regarding the building.

اِنَّ اللّٰهَ اشْتَرٰي مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِيْنَ اَنْفُسَهُمْ وَاَمْوَالَهُمْ بِاَنَّ لَهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ ۭ يُقَاتِلُوْنَ فِيْ سَبِيْلِ اللّٰهِ فَيَقْتُلُوْنَ وَيُقْتَلُوْنَ ۣ وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا فِي التَّوْرٰىةِ وَالْاِنْجِيْلِ وَالْقُرْاٰنِ وَمَنْ اَوْفٰي بِعَهْدِهٖ مِنَ اللّٰهِ فَاسْـتَبْشِرُوْا بِبَيْعِكُمُ الَّذِيْ بَايَعْتُمْ بِهٖ ۭ وَذٰلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيْمُ 

**The Meaning of the Covenant Pledged to the Prophet:** Now, He clarified the reality of that covenant which is pledged to the Prophet, and in which every Muslim shares by saying 'There is no deity but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah.' This is to place its requirements before everyone, so they can decide what conduct is contrary to it.

He stated: this covenant is a treaty of buying and selling between Allah and the believers, where the believers hand over their wealth and lives to Allah, and Allah promises them Paradise in return. We explained elsewhere that the highest honor of man is that despite being a creature, Allah enters into a treaty of buying and selling with him, taking a responsibility upon His own Self. This is because He granted man choice within a sphere, wishing that he submit his wealth and life to Him voluntarily, to receive His Paradise in return. 'They fight in the cause of Allah, so they kill and are killed.' After this treaty, no sincere believer can hold back his wealth or life; they strive in His cause, killing and being killed—attaining the rank of a warrior if they kill, and a martyr if they are killed. 'a promise [binding] upon Him in truth in the Torah and the Gospel and the Quran.' This promise of Paradise in return for sacrifice is a shared reality of all scriptures. For example, in the Torah:

'Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God is one Lord: And thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might.' (Deuteronomy 6:4-5).

In the Gospel:

'And every one that hath forsaken houses, or brethren, or sisters, or father, or mother, or wife, or children, or lands, for my name's sake, shall receive an hundredfold, and shall inherit everlasting life.' (Matthew 19:29).

In the Gospels, Paradise is often termed 'the kingdom of heaven'. In the Torah, the Jews turned these rewards into worldly blessings due to their greed; yet, the mention of eternal life is present in the prophets' odes.

'And who is truer to his covenant than Allah? So rejoice in your transaction which you have contracted. And that is what is the great success.' This encourages the Muslims to make sacrifices: who can be truer to His promise than Allah? Therefore, be delighted with this transaction you contracted. What you sold to Him, hand it over upon His demand, and be ready for what you are to receive. Allah will fulfill His promise. His Paradise is a great thing; this is no loss, but is receiving an eternal kingdom in exchange for a temporary life.

اَلتَّاۗىِٕبُوْنَ الْعٰبِدُوْنَ الْحٰمِدُوْنَ السَّآىِٕحُوْنَ الرّٰكِعُوْنَ السّٰجِدُوْنَ الْاٰمِرُوْنَ بِالْمَعْرُوْفِ وَالنَّاهُوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ وَالْحٰفِظُوْنَ لِحُدُوْدِ اللّٰهِ ۭ وَبَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِيْنَ 

**The Attributes of True Believers:** Just as the news is omitted in verse 107, it is omitted in this verse. This is also the view of Al-Zajjaj, which I find robust. Sometimes, the force of the discourse clarifies the news even if it is not expressed. The context shows that those characterized by these attributes are the true believers; give these believers the glad tiding.

**The Meaning of 'Siyahat' (Riyadh):** All the words here, except *sā'ihūn*, were discussed in their places. I faced difficulty in translating *sā'ihūn*. *Sāha/Yasīhu* means wandering on the earth, as in verse 2: 'So travel freely (*fasīhū*) throughout the land for four months.' But in this context, it carries its technical meaning. 'Wandering' (*siyāhat*) has been a technical term of religion since ancient times—meaning: going out into the land for worship and asceticism (*al-ḏahābu fī al-arḍi lil-ʿibādati wa-al-tarahhubi*). In ancient religions, this was given great significance: a person would leave his home, family, and the noise of the world to spend his time in remembering God, meditation, and spiritual exercises in deserts, mountains, and silent places, contenting himself with the minimum required to live. Buddhist monks, Christian ascetics, and Hindu yogis chose this. If they turned to people, it was only to deliver some words of goodness. This is what was termed *siyāhat*.

**The Limits of Wandering in Islam:** That part of *siyāhat* which is monasticism is prohibited in Islam, because Islam is the religion of nature; but that part which relates to asceticism, trust, meditation, spiritual exercises, searching for truth, seeking knowledge, calling to Islam, and Jihad is desired in Islam, and has been integrated into fasting, retreat (*i'tikāf*), Hajj, and Jihad. For this reason, we find both negation and confirmation regarding it: on one hand, 'No monasticism (*siyāhat*) in Islam'; on the other, 'The wandering of this Ummah is fasting and remaining in the mosques.' Abu Dawud records that a person sought permission to wander. The Prophet replied: 'The wandering of my Ummah is Jihad in the cause of Allah.' This shows that while the monastic aspect was excluded, the core purpose remains, encompassing fasting, retreat, emigration, Jihad, calling to Islam, and traveling to seek knowledge. This is for both men and women, as is clear from the word *sā'ihāt* in Surah At-Tahrim—excluding those tasks from which women are exempted by the Shariah, such as active fighting.

Generally, translators have translated it as 'those who fast' or 'those who travel in His cause.' But these translate only one aspect, whereas it carries multiple aspects. I have translated it as 'those who perform spiritual exercises' (*riyāḍ karney wāley*), which is nearer to the spirit of the word.

**A Mirror of Repentance Before the Hypocrites:** The preceding verse clarified the reality of the covenant; this verse describes the character of the believers, placing a mirror before the hypocrites so they can reform if they wish.

First, He mentioned 'repentance' (*tawbah*), which means turning to Allah. This is the first step of a servant, turning away from satanic paths to walk on His straight path, and this repentance supports him at every step, correcting his slips.

**Worship:** Then, He mentioned 'worship' (*ʿibādah*), which refers to the greatest right of God. The one who turns to God must fulfill His right of worship, uniquely for Him without partner, and obedience is its inseparable part.

**Praise:** Then, He mentioned 'praise' (*hamd*), which is the soul of all worship, because prayer and zakat are manifestations of gratitude and confession of blessings. Without this gratitude, no one can fulfill the right of worship, nor can there be any soul in it.

**Wandering:** Then, He mentioned 'wandering' (*siyāhat*), which was explained above. This is a comprehensive expression for all efforts, hardships, and spiritual exercises a person chooses to reform his outward and inward, learn and teach the religion, and elevate it, sacrificing his ease, desires, and joys.

**Private Prayers:** Then, He mentioned 'bowing and prostrating' (*al-rākiʿūna al-sāǧidūna*), which refers to private prayers. These prayers protect all those things mentioned above, giving life to the spiritual exercises.

**Enjoining Right and Forbidding Wrong:** Then, He mentioned enjoining right and forbidding wrong. The preceding points relate to individual reform; this relates to their role in society—namely: they enjoin right and forbid wrong, caring for the reform of others and performing their duty.

**Guarding the Limits of Allah:** Finally, He mentioned guarding the limits of Allah. This is the expression of piety, showing that they remain alert at all times so no limit of God is broken, committing no violation themselves nor permitting others to do so.

These aspects are described here to show the hypocrites what true believers are like, in contrast to those who claim faith while being contrary in character.

Commentary on Verses 113-129:

**Conclusion of the Surah:** These are the concluding verses of the Surah, addressed directly to the Muslims. After purifying the community of all corrupt elements, He gave several instructions and glad tidings. First, He instructed: it is not for the Prophet or the believers to ask forgiveness for the polytheists, even if they are relatives. In this connection, He clarified the true nature of Abraham's asking forgiveness for his father, so it would not cause confusion. This instruction aims to purify the believers of any connection to falsehood, so they live and die solely for the truth. If any familial bias remains in a person, it opens the way to hypocrisy and disbelief; hence, He closed this door completely.

Afterward, He gave glad tidings of acceptance of repentance to those who were anxious to reform after these warnings. Then, He advised the residents of Medina and the desert to remain with the truthful, so their company would reform their weaknesses, and to keep in mind that great reward He promised for every minor or major deed performed in His cause. Particularly, He advised the desert-dwellers to maintain connection with the center to benefit from the Prophet's company, while teaching their nation. Then, He commanded the Muslims to fight the disbelievers around them, being harsh upon them, while pointing to the fate of those who remained in hypocrisy. 

Finally, addressing the Muslims, He explained what a great favor they received in the Prophet's sending, urging them to appreciate it; and addressing those who turn away, He commanded the Prophet to say: I do not care, Allah is sufficient for me, He is my trust, and He is the Lord of the Great Throne. Recite the verses:

**Prohibiting Asking Forgiveness for the Polytheists:** 'It is not for the Prophet and those who have believed to ask forgiveness for the polytheists, even if they were relatives, after it has become clear to them that they are the companions of Hellfire.' This is an instruction to avoid the slightest trace of polytheism, which is a backdoor to hypocrisy—namely: asking forgiveness for those against whom the proof has been completed and the declaration of disassociation made, yet they did not believe. Out of relative-ship or love, asking mercy for such opponents means that relative-ship dominates the truth. By 'the polytheists' (*al-mushrikīn*) here, He refers to the polytheists of Arabia, from whom a declaration of disassociation has been made. To those hypocrites who refused to cut ties with them, He instructed: do not ask forgiveness for them. In a prophet's mission, a stage comes when he and the believers are prohibited from asking forgiveness for the disbelievers—indeed, a stage comes when they supplicate: 'My Lord, do not leave upon the earth from among the disbelievers an inhabitant.' This is the stage when Allah chooses to cut all ties of the believers with the disbelievers, so they face their punishment without any shield of security. We explained in Al-Anfal that the prayers of the Prophet and the believers serve as security. By prohibiting them from asking forgiveness, He deprived the polytheists of their final security. 'even if they were relatives' (*wa-law kānū ulī qurbā*) shows that relative-ship to the Prophet does not benefit anyone without faith; what then is the status of other relationships? 'after it has become clear to them that they are the companions of Hellfire' (*min ba'di mā tabayyana lahum annahum ashābu al-jahīm*) shows that once people are declared to be the companions of Hell, asking forgiveness for them is not permissible; but this does not prohibit supplicating for their guidance, because after the Prophet, there is no way to know whether someone will not believe. Thus, supplicating for their guidance is permissible. However, if a person died in disbelief, asking for his salvation is not permissible, because it negates the value of faith and His law of justice.

'And the request of forgiveness of Abraham for his father was only because of a promise he had made to him. But when it became apparent to him that he was an enemy to Allah, he disassociated himself from him. Indeed, Abraham was compassionate and patient.' **The Nature of Abraham's Request for His Father:**

This refutes a wrong assumption—namely: if Abraham (peace be upon him) asked forgiveness for his disbelieving father, why can we not do so for our relatives? He stated: Abraham did this solely to fulfill a promise he had made to his father, and when He informed him that his father was an enemy of Allah, he disassociated himself from him. The context indicates that He informed him that his father would not believe.

The promise referred to here is mentioned in several places in the Quran. In Surah Maryam: 'He said, "Peace be upon you. I will ask forgiveness for you of my Lord. Indeed, He is ever gracious to me."' (Maryam: 47). This shows the significance of fulfilling promises: if a person makes a promise—especially of prayer—he must fulfill it as long as it is permissible.

'Indeed, Abraham was compassionate and patient.' *Awwāh* means compassionate and gentle-hearted; *halīm* means patient. Describing him through these attributes shows that Allah liked his compassion and patience regarding his father. In the odes of Surah Maryam, it is mentioned that his father dealt with him with harshness, but the obedient son bore it with patience, promising to ask forgiveness for him. Reflect on how much pain a compassionate son must have felt when he had to disassociate himself from his father! But since Allah desires His servant to be both compassionate and firm in His cause, Abraham displayed both aspects, which is the model for those who serve the religion.

**A Warning to the Muslims:** 'And Allah would not let a people stray after He has guided them until He makes clear to them what they should avoid. Indeed, Allah is Knowing of all things.' While this refers to His general law—that He does not let anyone stray until He completes the proof and makes the truth clear, which is why those who oppose Him do not deserve to have others ask forgiveness for them—the words 'after He has guided them' (*ba'da iḏ hādāhum*) points to a warning to the Muslims: having guided you to the straight path, He has informed you of what you must avoid. Now, the responsibility lies on you to avoid these dangers. If you do not, you will go astray, and Allah leaves such people to their deviation. This emphasizes the prohibition of asking forgiveness for the polytheists. We explained above that maintaining ties with those whose hostility is clear can become a trial for a person, gradually leading him to prioritize relative-ship over principles and beliefs. Those who boast of their ancestry despite being Muslims, or are proud of past tyrants instead of Muhammad bin Qasim, have fallen into this trial. 'Indeed, Allah is Knowing of all things' is part of this warning—meaning: God is aware of the hidden, and if anyone harbors any connection to polytheism, it will not remain hidden from Him.

'Indeed, to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth; He gives life and causes death. And you have not besides Allah any protector or helper.' This is an ode of monotheism, emphasizing the preceding subject, so any trace of polytheism is ended.

*Tāba/Yatūbu* means to turn, but when attributed to Allah and followed by the preposition *'alā*, it carries the meaning of His mercy and accepting the repentance of His servants.

**General Glad Tiding of the Acceptance of Repentance:** 'Allah has already forgiven the Prophet and the Emigrants and the Ansar who followed him in the hour of difficulty after the hearts of a party of them had almost deviated; then He forgave them. Indeed, He was to them Kind and Merciful.' This is the general glad tiding of the acceptance of repentance that came down like rain upon those who proved themselves worthy after these warnings. We indicated that this Surah purified the entire society, separating the corrupt elements from the sincere. Those who were sincere washed away any temporary dust on their hearts through their tears of repentance, and Allah gave them the glad tiding of acceptance.

**The Position of Mentioning the Prophet:** The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is mentioned at the very top of this list, because he was the best of this society. His mention does not imply that he committed any error of the nature committed by others, but points to that soft attitude he chose regarding the hypocrites, mentioned in 'Allah forgive you, why did you permit them...', which He encompassed in His mercy. We explained elsewhere that since prophets are the criteria of truth, Allah rebukes them and refines their actions even when their concessions arise from their good desires, if these exceed the standard He chose for them.

**The Order of Ranks in the Islamic Society:** Next, He mentioned the Muhajirun and the Ansar. This describes the order of ranks in the Islamic society: the highest rank belongs to the Muhajirun, and then to the Ansar. This also shows that the foundation of ranks in this society is not family, lineage, or wealth, but following the Messenger and serving Islam. Those who are foremost in these are the highest in rank, regardless of their family or wealth.

'who followed him in the hour of difficulty' (*alladhīna ittaba'ūhu fī sā'ati al-'usrah*) points fundamentally to Tabuk, which was fought under highly difficult circumstances, but also points generally to those difficulties and trials the Muhajirun and Ansar faced from the very beginning. This references their trial to show that true faith is what has been tested on the criteria of difficulties. It also shows that this characteristic of their faith served as their intercession to receive His mercy and forgiveness. Allah does not deprive His loyal servants of His facilitation. When they commit some error, He sowed the seeds of repentance in their hearts, they repent, and He accepts their repentance. He overlooks only those who claim love with their tongues, but avoid facing difficulties in His cause. 'after the hearts of a party of them had almost deviated' (*min ba'di mā kāda yazīġu qulūbu farīqin minhum*) shows that the vast majority of the Muhajirun and Ansar remained firm through all difficulties. Only a small group committed some weakness at Tabuk, but they woke after being warned. Then, they turned to Allah, and Allah accepted their repentance.

**Glad Tidings to Those Whose Decision Was Postponed:**

وَعَلَي الثَّلٰثَةِ الَّذِيْنَ خُلِّفُوْا ۭ حَتّٰي اِذَا ضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْاَرْضُ بِمَا رَحُبَتْ وَضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ اَنْفُسُهُمْ وَظَنُّوْٓا اَنْ لَّمْ مَلْجَاَ مِنَ اللّٰهِ اِلَّآ اِلَيْهِ ۭ ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوْبُوْا ۭ اِنَّ اللّٰهَ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيْمُ

After the general glad tiding, this is the glad tiding to those three whose decision was postponed in verse 106. 'until, when the earth was straitened for them in spite of its vastness and their souls was straitened...'—meaning: when they felt that brokenness and anxiety of heart which attracts His mercy, Allah accepted their repentance. 'the earth was straitened for them in spite of its vastness' points to what they faced: narrations show that they spent fifty days with everyone turning away from them, including their relatives, companions, and wives. Medina was the same, its vastness and blessings were the same, and the brotherhood and love sowed by Islam was at its height; but these three found no one looking at them because they were under the displeasure of Allah and His Messenger. The earth is vast for a person as long as the hearts of people are open to him; but when they close, the earth becomes a prison. For these three, the land of Medina became like a prison, and they spent fifty days in this state. 'and their souls was straitened' (*wa-dāqat 'alayhim anfusuhum*) describes their inner state: just as the outward world closed upon them, their own inner self closed upon them. Sometimes, even if the outward world is closed, if a person is satisfied with his conduct, he does not feel anxious, finding some peace in his inner self; but these three could not bear the displeasure of Allah and His Messenger for a single moment, let alone spending fifty days while the walls of Medina bore witness to His displeasure. For those possessing faith, what grief can be greater than this? Consequently, they became averse to their own existence. Reflect on their plight: no hope from the outside, and no peace from within. 'and they certain that there is no refuge from Allah except in Him' describes their heart's state: despite the severity of the check, they turned to God with desperation, knowing that only He can save them from His apprehension. Thus, narrations show that two of them remained in their homes, weeping and asking forgiveness day and night. The third, Ka'b bin Malik, would go out only hoping to find some fragrance of His pleasure. If they harbored any hypocrisy, when they were rejected by the Prophet, they would have sought another refuge; but they were of true faith; therefore, like a child who clings to his mother when rebuked, they ran to God to escape His punishment, presenting no false excuse nor conspiring with others. Indeed, during those days, the messenger of the King of Ghassan brought a letter to Ka'b bin Malik: 'We have heard that your companion has treated you with harshness; you are not a person to be wasted; come to us, we will honor you.' But he threw the letter into the furnace, because he believed that only God's mercy can save him from His apprehension, and the King of Ghassan could not save him. This belief is the soul of faith and the core of monotheism. With this belief, when a person turns to God in His apprehension, His mercy rises to accept him. 'Then He turned to them so they might repent. Indeed, Allah is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful'—meaning: when their hearts were broken through these difficulties, Allah looked upon them with mercy, granting them the facilitation of sincere repentance. *Li-yatūbū* is used in its true sense. As passed, they had confessed their sins before, but their repentance was not accepted then, because—as we indicated—relying on their past services, they did not feel the gravity of their error as they were supposed to, nor did they feel that brokenness of heart which was required of them. A person is evaluated based on his rank; everyone's loyalty is not measured on the same scale. Allah chose to test them so they and other believers would learn what brokenness of heart is required for true repentance.

**Repentance Begins From Allah:** 'He turned to them so they might repent' (*tāba 'alayhim li-yatūbū*) reveals that repentance begins from Allah. He sowed the desire of turning to Him in the servant's heart, and when the servant repents, He accepts it. Allah created human nature such that if he possesses faith, his heart is grieved by his sins, and a feeling of regret arises within him to turn to his Lord. If he acts upon this feeling, Allah also guides his tongue and heart to those words of repentance which He likes and accepts. Only those who have their conscience and faith dead are deprived of this.

**Instruction to Choose the Company of the Truthful:**

يٰٓاَيُّھَا الَّذِيْنَ اٰمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللّٰهَ وَكُوْنُوْا مَعَ الصّٰدِقِيْنَ

Having purified the community, He gave several instructions to keep themselves pure of disbelief and hypocrisy in the future. The first instruction is: fear Allah, and choose the company and partnership of the truthful and sincere. Company plays a major role in a person's reform or corruption. If a person spends his time with disbelievers, hypocrites, and the ignorant, even a strong person will eventually be affected by them. Similarly, the company of those firm in faith and deeds produces resolve within a weak person to overcome his weaknesses, gradually becoming like them. Because of the significance of company, those in the land of disbelief were commanded to emigrate to the land of Islam, and those in the desert were instructed to send groups to Medina to learn and remain connected with the Prophet and his companions. From what passed regarding the hypocrites and bards, it was clear that their disease arose largely from their environment. Thus, among the precautions, the primary one was: fear Allah and choose the company of the truthful. The fear of God is the internal guardian, and the company of the truthful supports him against Satan. 'The truthful' (*al-sādiqīn*) is used in its true sense—meaning: those whose words and deeds completely align, which is the contrary of the hypocrites.

Commentary on Verses 120-121:

**Further Emphasis of the Preceding Subject:** 'It was not for the dwellers of Medina and those desert-dwellers around them to remain behind the Messenger of Allah or to prefer their own lives over his life.' This is further emphasis of the instruction to choose the company of the truthful, encouraging the weaker ones: although this path is filled with difficulties, trials, and dangers, for every difficulty, hunger, or grief you suffer in His cause, and for every blow you strike at the enemy, a righteous deed is written for you, and on the Day of Recompense, everyone will receive the best reward. The meaning is: since Allah's dealing with those who suffer in His cause is of this nature, it was not fitting for the dwellers of Medina and the desert to avoid supporting the Prophet, prioritizing their lives over his life. Nor is it permissible for anyone in the future to turn away from the truth and its standard-bearers out of fear of dangers. 'Indeed, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of those who do good'—the word 'doers of good' (*muhsinīn*) points to the condition for these deeds to be accepted: that they be performed solely for His pleasure, empty of any other concern. 'nor do they tread any ground that angers the disbelievers' refers to those actions performed to discourage the enemy and establish the believers' awe.

**The Cure for Hypocrisy:** 'And it is not for the believers to go forth all at once. For there should separate from every division of them a group to obtain understanding in the religion and warn their people when they return to them that they might be cautious.' This also relates to choosing the company of the truthful. In verse 97 above, it passed that the desert-dwellers did not benefit from the Prophet's company, which is why they are ignorant of the limits of Shariah, and the disease of hypocrisy easily seizes them. Now, He described the cure: they were supposed to find a way to benefit from the Prophet's company. If all could not leave their homes, why did they not arrange to send a group from every bstee to learn the religion in the Prophet's company, and then return to teach their nation? Thus, they could care for their business and herds, while benefiting from the Prophet's company. Narrations show that some Companions had arranged to take turns visiting the Prophet, so they could care for their business and benefit from his company.

**The Purpose of Education in Islam:** The word used for learning is 'to obtain understanding in the religion' (*li-yatafaqqahū fī al-dīn*), which means obtaining faim and insight in the religion; and the word used for teaching is 'to warn' (*li-yundhirū*), which means warning and waking people for the Hereafter. These two words are chosen based on the true purpose of education in Islam: obtaining insight in the religion and training others for the Hereafter. Other things are secondary and subject to this target.

'O you who have believed, fight those adjacent to you of the disbelievers and let them find in you harshness. And know that Allah is with those who fear Him.' This verse is the summary of the entire Surah. In this Surah, as you saw, after completing the proof, He declared disassociation and war against the polytheists and the People of the Book. Under verses 23-24, it passed that the hypocrites avoided cutting ties and fighting those adjacent to them due to their relationships and interests. Having exposed their hypocrisy and explained the true requirements of faith, He placed the core demand before them again. Although the address is general, the focus is on them. He stated: fight those adjacent to you of the disbelievers. Those adjacent to you, just as they were most deserving of your call, are now most deserving of your swords after the declaration of war. Those who show weakness out of relative-ship or interests wrong their own souls, as stated in verse 23, and are threatened in verse 24: wait until Allah delivers His command. Sincere faith is born when a person is ready to fight his relatives for the sake of the truth. 'adjacent to you' (*yalūnakum*) highlights this. 'and let them find in you harshness'—meaning: let them feel that no alliance or love remains within you for them; rather, you must be their enemies collectively, just as they are your enemies. Up to now, they found a soft corner, expecting to exploit you. This must end.

'And know that Allah is with those who fear Him' carries two aspects. First, to encourage the believers: do not show weakness; Allah's help accompanies His righteous servants. If you remain firm on piety, victory is yours. God's companionship is His help. Second, to warn the Muslims: in fighting and being harsh, observe the limits of Allah, committing no transgression; Allah is only with those who fear Him. This emphasizes the instructions given previously: keeping the terms of treaties for those who kept them, and respecting the sacred months.

Commentary on Verses 124-127:

**The Conduct of the Hard-Core Hypocrites:** 'And whenever a surah is revealed, there are among them those who say, "Which of you has this increased in faith?" As for those who have believed, it has increased them in faith, and they rejoice.' This refers to those hard-core hypocrites whose hypocrisy grew harder and harder despite these warnings, and they died in this state. 'And whenever a surah is revealed...'—'surah' refers to any part or portion of the Quran.

These hypocrites came to the Prophet's assemblies only for show, where they faced new demands of the Quran daily. These demands were highly heavy upon them. They did not dare speak against them openly, but chose to utter sarcastic remarks to vent their anger—asking: 'Which of you has this increased in faith?' Through this, they sought to degrade the commands and discourage the sincere believers. He stated: for true believers, every surah and command increases their faith, and they are delighted to hear it, because it is like rain; but for those with disease in their hearts, 'it adds filth to their filth and they die while they are disbelievers.' They never find the facilitation of repentance, and die in disbelief.

**The Effect of the Quran on the Hypocrites:** Having the Quran increase the believers' faith and add filth to the hypocrites' is a reality. Every command opens a field of trial for the believers, and when they win it, their resolve is strengthened to win other fields, until they reach the highest stage of success; but the hypocrites face defeat after defeat, until their resolve is gone, and their hearts are sealed with layers of hypocrisy that stifle all their capacities.

'Do they not see that they are tried once or twice every year? Yet they do not repent, nor do they remember.' This explains why they are deprived of repentance: if they reflected, they would realize that their disease has reached the stage where the doors of repentance are closed. Allah desires mercy for His servants, which is why He tests everyone once or twice a year with some trial to wake them. Those blessed with facilitation learn a lesson, but those deprived of it do not. They are now in this state: no warning wakes them. 'Yet they do not repent' (*thumma lā yatūbūna*) points to their hard-heartedness, and 'nor do they remember' (*lā hum yaḏ-ḏakkarūna*) points to their blunt intellect, because repentance is the deed of the heart, and remembering is of the intellect. Their deeds have darkened both.

'And whenever a surah is revealed, they look at each other, [saying], "Does anyone see you?" Then they dismiss themselves. Allah has dismissed their hearts because they are a people who do not understand.' This is a picture of how they sneak away from the Prophet's assemblies. When the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) summoned the Muslims to prepare for some expedition or to inform them of some instructions, the hypocrites came only for show; but when they realized that some difficult task was ahead, requiring their share, they made signs to one another: 'Does anyone see you?' Then, they sneaked away, taking cover of others. This is detailed in Surah An-Nur:

'The believers are only those who believe in Allah and His Messenger and, when they are with him on a matter of common interest, do not depart until they have asked his permission. Indeed, those who ask your permission, those are the ones who believe in Allah and His Messenger. So when they ask your permission for some of their affairs, then grant permission to whom you will of them and ask forgiveness for them of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. Do not make [your] calling of the Messenger among yourselves as the call of one of you to another. Already Allah knows those of you who slip away, hiding behind others. So let those beware who dissent from his order, lest fitnah strike them or a painful punishment strike them.' (An-Nur: 62-63).

'Allah has turned [i.e., dismissed] their hearts because they are a people who do not understand'—when they turned away from Allah and His Messenger, Allah turned their hearts away. This is His curse, and carries the same meaning as 'when they deviated, Allah made their hearts deviate.' This sealing of hearts is the result of their own deeds. 'because they are a people who do not understand'—He turns the capacities of those who do not use them to learn after His warnings toward the direction they chose.

Commentary on Verses 128-129:

'There has certainly come to you a Messenger from among yourselves. Grievous to him is what you suffer; [he is] concerned over you and to the believers is kind and merciful.' These two verses are revealed as a final warning. First, He pointed to that great favor He showed by sending the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Although the address is general, the focus is on those who, instead of appreciating this favor, consider it a calamity. He stated: a Messenger has come to you from among yourselves. The aspects of completing the proof and favor in 'from among yourselves' (*min anfusikum*) were discussed under Surah Al-Baqarah. 'Grievous to him is what you suffer' (*ʿazīzun ʿalayhi mā ʿanittum*)—meaning: while those of wretchedness assume the Prophet is a source of calamities, in reality, whatever causes you suffering in this world and the Hereafter is highly heavy upon his heart. He desires to save you from all destructions, and his entire effort is for this purpose. But those who consider this world as the only life find his commands heavy because they are contrary to their interests; whereas if they knew the significance of the Hereafter, they would realize that his anxiety is to save them from grief. 'concerned over you' (*ḥarīṣun ʿalaykum*)—meaning: he is concerned for you, empty of any personal interest; he desires your faith, reform, and success, just as a father desires the well-being of his children, never being satisfied. Thus, woe to those who do not appreciate such a compassionate being. 'and to the believers is kind and merciful'—the difference between *ra'ūf* (kind) and *rahīm* (merciful) was discussed elsewhere: one denotes preventing evil, while the other denotes granting good. Both are among the beautiful attributes of Allah, which are used here for the Prophet, showing that in his compassion, he was a reflection of the attributes of Allah. This segment explains 'concerned over you,' which is why no coordinating conjunction is used, to show complete connection.

'But if they turn away, say, "Sufficient for me is Allah; there is no deity except Him. On Him I have relied, and He is the Lord of the Great Throne."' This addresses the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): if these ungrateful people do not appreciate this great good He revealed for them, it is their own ruin, not yours. Your concern is for their own benefit. If they had appreciated it, they would have reformed their own life; but they do not understand, so do not worry about them. Command them: Allah is sufficient for me. That is: my concern for you was for your own sake. If you turn away, do not assume I will remain helpless. My protector, support, and power is Allah, besides whom there is no other deity. My trust is in Him, and He is the Lord of the Great Throne; and the one whose trust is in the Lord of the Great Throne has no concern for the ingratitude of others.

These are the final lines of the commentary of this Surah written down today, Friday, at 9:00 AM. May Allah forgive the errors and grant place in the hearts for the true words. And our final call is: Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.
